The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt — A World That Refuses to Let You Go

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | 30+ Hours In — And I'm Just Getting Started
Thirty hours. That's how long I've sunk into The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt, and if the achievement counter is anything to go by (13 out of 78 — yes, I checked), I've barely scratched the surface. That alone tells you everything about the sheer scale of this game.
From the moment you step into the boots of Geralt of Rivia, the world doesn't just open up — it breathes. The wind tears through golden wheat fields, NPCs argue in taverns without waiting for you to listen, and every notice board leads you down a rabbit hole you didn't see coming.
The quests here aren't filler. Even the so-called "side" content carries emotional weight that most games never achieve in their main storylines. I've lost track of how many times I paused just to absorb the writing.
Combat clicks satisfyingly once you stop button-mashing and start reading your enemies. Signs, oils, timing — it rewards patience.
The verdict? Thirty hours feels like a prologue. With 65 achievements still locked and entire regions left unexplored, this game isn't something you finish — it's something you live in for a while.
Recommended without hesitation.

I went into The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt knowing basically nothing about the books or the lore and somehow came out feeling like I could write a 50 page essay on it (not that I will, don’t worry). This game has officially become both my new and old favorite open-world action RPG at the same time, which doesn’t make sense, but neither does half the stuff Geralt deals with, so it fits.

First off, how does a game that’s over 10 years old still look this good? The world is ridiculously detailed, my pc is average but still it was capable of giving me high quality experience on 1440p high/very high (80fps in game). Also it’s one of those rare games where exploring doesn’t feel like a chore but more like, “oh cool, what weird cursed wierd ass thing am I about to find now?”

The combat feels great. It’s not the absolute best ever made, but it’s satisfying enough that you actually want to fight things instead of running past them. Plus, you get to spin around with swords like a professional monster blender, which never gets old.

And then there’s Gwent. Honestly, I came here to hunt monsters and ended up addicted to a card game (I even played it irl). At some point I was less concerned about saving the world and more focused on building the ultimate Gwent deck.

The story and lore was incredible. What I really liked is that the game doesn’t just dump everything on you, it kind of makes you work for it. The lore is like a puzzle that slowly comes together while you’re playing. You’ll see events happening, meet characters, and only later realize how important they actually were. Like with Uma. at first you’re just like “what is this little gremlin?” and later it hits you: oh. OH. That’s actually huge. If you know, you know.

What really ties all of this together is the dialogue. This game basically runs on conversations, and somehow they never feel boring. Every choice you make in dialogue can actually matter. whether it’s for the lore, side quests, relationships, or even the ending. Sometimes you think you’re just picking a random line, and 10 hours later the game hits you with the consequences (foreshadowing). Characters like Keira Metz are a perfect example. one conversation can completely change her fate depending on what you say. It makes you actually care about what you pick instead of just skipping through lines like in other games.

Even the side quests are heavily driven by dialogue, and that’s what makes them so good. They’re not just “go here, kill that” they feel like real stories with real outcomes and the fact that these choices can affect the ending of the entire game is kind of insane for a 2015 game that is still on top in 2026. You’re not just playing the story. you’re kinda shaping it, sometimes without even realizing it.

Even without knowing anything beforehand, the game does an amazing job of teaching you about its world naturally. By the end, I felt connected to the characters and understood what was going on without ever needing to Google stuff every five minutes.

Geralt himself was an absolute legend. Although I’m not sure if it’s just me, but the man runs a little… funny. Still, he somehow has enough charisma (or “aura” in my term) to rizz up basically everyone he meets (and sometimes have "fun" (; with them), which is impressive considering his main hobbies are fighting monsters and being emotionally unavailable.

Overall, The Witcher 3 is one of those games that completely pulls you in and doesn’t let go. It’s funny, dark, weird, emotional, and occasionally makes you question your life and why you’re playing cards in the middle of a war, but that’s part of the charm.

[h1]The Witcher 3 Review - 65 Hours Later[/h1]

[i]"Glad I played it 10 years later, because if I played it earlier, my other games would be boring."[/i]

[b]What impressed me the most:[/b]
[list]
[*]Side quests that are better written than some full games
[*]Choices that actually feel meaningful instead of fake "illusion of choice" nonsense
[*]World-building that feels alive, dirty, tragic, and believable
[*]Characters that stay in your head long after the quest ends
[*]Soundtrack that quietly manipulates your soul like a medieval emotional support wizard
[/list]

Combat has flaws, movement can feel clunky at times, and Roach occasionally operates using advanced quantum physics, but somehow none of that ruined the experience.

This game didn’t feel like "content."
It felt like an adventure.

Wind's howling
The Witcher 3 Wild Hunt isn't just an RPG  it’s a benchmark for storytelling in the gaming industry. Geralt of Rivia’s journey takes you through political intrigue, monster hunting and deeply emotional family drama all while making you feel the weight of every gray decision you make.

The Pros
Incredible Depth: Even a simple notice board contract can turn into a 2-hour epic with a moral twist you didn't see coming.
Memorable Characters: From Yennefer and my dear Triss to the Bloody Baron, every character has depth, flaws, and a soul.
Gwent: You will likely spend 20 hours ignoring the apocalypse just to collect rare cards and play a "round of Gwent."
Art Direction: Even years later, watching the sunset over the fields of Velen or the peaks of Skellige is breathtaking

The Cons
Inventory management and looting can feel a bit cluttered.
Roach still has a mind of her own and occasionally defies the laws of physics.

The core gameplay is simple but rich. You and your opponent play over up to three rounds, each trying to have the higher total strength at the end of a round. First to win two rounds takes the match. You build a deck of up to ten cards plus a leader, then take turns playing cards from your hand onto one of three rows: melee, ranged, or siege. The strategic layer comes from special cards, row effects, and clever synergies that force you to think several turns ahead.
There are five distinct factions, each with its own personality and optimal playstyle. Northern Realms rewards tight combos and morale boosts. Nilfgaard excels at control, spying, and disrupting the opponent. Scoia'tael is flexible and punishes overcommitment to single rows. Monsters thrive on weather effects and resilient units that keep coming back. Skellige brings aggressive discard and resurrection mechanics that can swing games dramatically.
Weather cards can blanket entire rows and cripple your opponent. Scorch destroys the strongest units on the board. Spies let you play to the enemy side while drawing extra cards. Decoys, horns, tight bonds, and leader abilities all create constant meaningful decisions. Every match feels different depending on the decks and how the draws go.
Deck building is satisfying and rewarding. You can chase pure power stacks, weather control, spy disruption, or resurrection combos. Finding and adding strong cards feels great, and every faction has multiple viable archetypes. The balance is solid overall, with interesting counters between factions.
The presentation is fantastic. Every card has beautiful artwork, and the audio feedback when playing cards is crisp and immersive.

phenomenal. even for an 11-year-old game, it is still an incredible experience. this is my first exposure to the Witcher IP and it's left an unforgettable mark on me. the characters are very compelling, with dialogue and interactions that are enjoyable, funny, and memorable.  the story is very well-written, the action is thrilling, the romance and comedy is very fun and enjoyable, and the game does a good job of carrying you through the story as a standalone title, as in that even with no prior knowledge to the lore of the previous games or the books, you can easily follow the story with minimal confusion. exposition is also very well done, in no sequence did i ever feel like a conversation happened just for the sake of telling the player this and that and it informed the players of the happenings in the world in a natural way, one particular sequence i liked a lot was the conversation with ambassador Var Attre in Yennefer's room after the audience with Emhyr, it was a very fluid way of telling newcomers to the franchise of where the politics of the continent sat and it did so through a conversation that would make sense to exist in the dialogue.

while i do have some bones to pick with the gameplay (ahem, horse handling), they're very minor setbacks to an otherwise timeless classic. although feeling slightly dated, the combat is still very satisfying and intuitive, and difficulty is easily customisable, you can make it as hard or easy as you want the game to be. 

anybody with any sort of preference over a video game will enjoy it regardless. whether you prefer action packed gameplay or slow, deliberate, storytelling, the game allows any and all kinds of players to experience the game how they see fit. and with it now being 11-years-old and frequently going on sale, the cheap price point for such an amazing piece of art is a blessing. overall it's a deserved GotY and a timeless classic, massive replayability and heaps of quality side content. its a one size fits all kind of game, a jack of all trades, but arguably even a master of some.

I first saw The Witcher 3 on a streamer's channel back in 2016. I'd never even heard of the series before that. Something about it caught my eye, so I decided to start from the very beginning - The Witcher 1. Played through the first game, then the second, and eventually made my way to the third. And what a journey it was.

I played through the game twice before I even bought it on Steam. Eventually I decided to buy it here, and I've now finished it a third time. Honestly, I'm already thinking about a fourth playthrough. The atmosphere in this game is just something else - it pulls you in and doesn't let go.

Every game in the trilogy has incredible soundtracks, but the first and third games really stand out. "River of Life" from the first game is beautiful, "A Quiet Corner" from the second sets the perfect mood, and the third game has some of my all-time favorites - "Kaer Morhen" and "A Story You Won't Believe," which always plays during Gwent matches and certain quests. Those tracks alone make you want to just stop and soak it all in.

Speaking of Gwent - it became one of my favorite games on its own. I'd sit there playing round after round.

The DLCs deserve a special mention. Blood and Wine is something else entirely. After spending so much time in the cold, harsh North, stepping into Toussaint feels like a breath of fresh air. The colors, the vineyards, the whole vibe - it's stunning. I keep coming back to it again and again. Hearts of Stone is great too - packed with interesting quests.

If you haven't played this yet, do yourself a favor and give it a shot. Start from the first game if you can. It's worth every minute.

After playing The Witcher 3, I honestly think it’s one of the closest things to a perfect RPG. The story is amazing, the visuals still look great even today, and the gameplay mechanics are really satisfying overall.

One issue I had was the input delay at times, which could feel frustrating during combat or movement, but it wasn’t enough to ruin the experience for me.

The world, characters, and atmosphere made the game unforgettable, and it’s definitely one of the best RPGs I’ve ever played. I honestly can’t wait for the next DLC and The Witcher 4 to come out.

Overall, I’d give it a solid 9/10.

 I want to talk about two things, combat and narrative. 

 If you don't engage in the magic and alchemy systems you'll probably think that combat is just bad. This is not Dark  Souls or Devil May Cry, it's about planning, using the right tools for situation, etc.
 Don't go in with the wrong expectations. 

 The narrative is where the game really shines. It's presented well, engaging and actually full of difficult choices. 
 Oftentimes it's about choosing the lesser evil. Do you trust the trapped spirit to save those children? If it does, what else might happen?
 Sometimes you wouldn't be able to predict the potential outcome at all, but not in a bs kinda way. You can only act in good faith and hope for the best, just like irl. 

 One time I couldn't bear the consequences and actually reloaded the game. Guess what, the outcome of the other choice was even worse for me, I was "right" all along. It's that kind of game. 

 Choices made early in the playthrough can have consequences way later. Sometimes it's just an additional scene for worldbuilding, other times it has direct impact on your quest flow. 

 Considering how cheap it is on sale I'd recommend it to everyone even remotely interested.
 P.S. don't worry about the dlcs, the game is massive as is.

What can I possibly say about The Witcher 3.

Although a lot of people say they had a hard time getting into the game, this was not the case for me at all. From the very first moment, I was hooked. Opening at Kaer Morhen with Yennefer, Vesemir, and Ciri, I immediately loved how the tutorial section was handled. It felt natural and engaging, and I was instantly drawn in.

Then I arrived in White Orchard. I was genuinely stunned by the area, especially knowing that this was just the “tutorial” zone. I remember feeling overwhelmed in the best way possible - the size of the map, the number of side quests, the points of interest. Other mechanics amazed me: swimming, diving, galloping with Roach, chasing enemies on horseback. I was immediately engaged.

After finishing almost everything in White Orchard, I moved on to Velen, and that’s when it really hit me. Everything I experienced before was now expanded on a much larger scale. The main quests, side quests, exploration, it was incredible. I loved exploring Velen, the swamps, and then the shift in atmosphere when arriving in Oxenfurt and Novigrad. Later, Skellige and Kaer Morhen continued to build on that sense of scale and variety.

I was completely immersed in every aspect of the gameplay. I loved reading the character entries for everyone I met and using the bestiary to prepare for monster fights. At one point, I switched to Death March difficulty to fully engage with the game’s systems, and that made me fall in love with it even more. I was truly playing as a witcher: reading the bestiary, applying oils, using potions and decoctions, combining signs with fast and strong attacks. The combat felt incredibly satisfying, and I enjoyed experimenting with different playstyles, whether more combat-focused, alchemy-heavy, or sign-based. The soundtrack is fantastic as well, and plays a huge role in immersing you in the world, enhancing every moment.

At some point, I realized I was so immersed in the world and story that I wanted to understand the lore more deeply. Geralt is a pre-existing character, and the world is rich with history, politics, and dark fantasy elements. I started watching lore videos, but then it clicked - I could just read the books myself. Once I started reading The Last Wish, everything fell into place. I understood Geralt more deeply, and it added another layer to my experience. Reading the books alongside playing the game was incredibly special, each medium enhanced the other. I really admire what CD Projekt Red has done with this world. It truly feels like a love letter to the Witcher universe.

At some point early on, I also realised how much the quest design and choices matter in this game. In one of the first major storylines, I made what felt like the “wrong” choice, and the outcome genuinely made me feel a bit depressed - I had to sit with that decision. When I looked into the alternative, I understood that it wasn’t about right or wrong, but about the kind of world The Witcher is. Later, when I read the story behind Geralt’s title as the “Butcher of Blaviken” in The Last Wish, it all clicked into place. This is a world without clear good or bad outcomes, where you’re often forced to choose the lesser evil. That made me come to terms with my choices and appreciate even more how the game encourages you to truly roleplay Geralt.

In terms of content, apart from the main questline, I completed all monster contracts and most side quests, apart from activities like horse racing and fist fights, which didn’t interest me as much. But I’m far from done. I’ve already started the first expansion, Hearts of Stone, and I’m amazed so far, and I’m very excited to continue with Blood and Wine. I’m also planning to keep exploring points of interest, especially as I now need more coin to upgrade my gear and eventually work toward Grandmaster sets.

This game completely absorbed me. The world, the characters, the systems, the storytelling, everything came together in a way that made it very hard to step away from. Easily one of my absolute favorite gaming experiences.

I finally gave The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt a replay, and I get why people still talk about it years later. The world feels alive in a way most open-world games struggle to achieve. Every village, forest, and swamp has its own story, often darker and more nuanced than expected.

What really sold me is the writing. Even side quests feel meaningful, with choices that don’t have obvious “good” or “bad” outcomes. Combat took a bit of getting used to, but once it clicked, mixing swordplay with signs and alchemy became genuinely satisfying.

Visually, it still holds up surprisingly well, especially with the updated Next-Gen. And the soundtrack quietly pulls you deeper into the atmosphere without being overbearing.

Overall, I’m glad I decided to play it. It’s not just a game you finish, it’s one you experience. Definitely worth the time if you enjoy story-driven RPGs.

This game is truly special. In every imaginable aspect.
The worldbuilding is insanely good, which shouldn't surprise anybody considering there is a worldwide famous book series behind it.
The soundtrack has so many bangers that it's hard to choose what to listen to, my favourites were the songs played in the taverns.
Combat, while probably the weakest part of the game is still pretty decent. Nothing special but it gets the job done and is still quite enjoyable (most of the time).
The biggest strength of this game is probably in its side content. The two DLCs are among the best DLCs ever made, and there are many memorable side quests that would certainly outclass main quests in other games. Also worth mentioning is Gwent, another part of side content that defines this game. It's not an everyday occurrence that a minigame is so popular they make a whole game out of it (even if it had to shut down, that's still a pretty good achievement).
All in all this is one of the best games ever made and definitely one my favourites.

Replayed on PC. CDPR's claim-to-fame and a game that has a riveting story, excellent side quests, a fantastic soundtrack, and a strong cast of side characters that is largely only brought down by its gameplay.

Pros:
- Story
- Cast of Characters
- Graphics & Art Direction
- OST
- Side Quest Variety
- Key Choices & Outcomes
- Ending Variety
- Gwent
- Hearts of Stone Expansion
- Blood & Wine Expansion

Mixed:
- Enemy/Boss Variety
- Busy Work (Map gets filled with ‘?’ symbols far too easily and largely uninteresting) 
- Ciri Point System for Endings
- Technical Fidelity (random frame drops for extended periods)

Cons:
- Combat System (Repetitive, boring, and easy)
- Build Variety (Not much depth but the DLC's help)
- Final Boss

There's not much to be said about the game that isn't widely known already so I'll place what I consider its 3 best features: (1) Main quest, (2) side quests, (3) cast of characters. The main questline is handled well both from a pacing perspective and its distinct 'acts'. It is fluid in how it handles blending the main story in with interesting characters with some of the best side quests in the game. The key decisions you can make help too as more often than not, the decisions are handled well.

As for flaws, while I could nitpick with the bloated loot system or standard open-world busy work (i.e. scavenger hunts/monster camps), TW3's main issue (just like The Witcher 1 & 2) is the combat, in many aspects. The perk system and build variety isn't that interesting, hitting enemies feels basic, and the same strategy can be used to take care of bosses and enemies alike. While there are some good parts to the enemy variety where you have to handle a few types differently with different types of Signs or parry a person with a shield, you still fight the same way 90% of the time. The expansions help to make builds have a bit more depth and give greater freedom in what armor you can use or magic to specialize in to change the combat some. 

As for the expansions, they’re both high-quality, as most anyone would agree. Hearts of Stone is linear with an emphasis on story, characters, and handful of interesting scenarios with a very memorable villain. It adds a little bit of filler on the map and runes, which help to address the weak build variety in the base game while also enhancing a build you already have made. Blood & Wine gives a high-quality adventure into a vibrant new area that excels in most respects, as its only real issue to me was the optional busy-work as it was just like the base game. Still, these expansions are some of the best out there so they're an easy recommend.

Overall, amazing game. PC has some added technical issues over consoles but it also gives some more freedom as well so I'm glad I replayed it. If the sequel can make the combat be more satisfying with greater depth and reduce some of the open-world tedium when doing everything in the game, it may be the best one yet. I'll rate TW3 a bit higher due to the stellar dlc's. 9.5/10.

As a fan of [i]The Witcher 2[/i], I knew I had to see how my choices played out in [b]The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt[/b]. It delivers an even grittier, more cinematic experience—this time with the massive, seamless open world that truly feels like a consequence of the wars I witnessed in the previous game. The story, following Geralt's search for Ciri, is gripping and deeply personal, but it’s the ghosts of my past decisions that make it feel unique to me.

That said, the save file import was the major talking point for me. I was initially skeptical about how much my specific path would be recognized—until I realized how much it reminded me of the branching consequences in [i]Detroit: Become Human[/i]. Having walked Vernon Roche’s path in [i]Assassins of Kings[/i], meeting him again in the backstreets of Novigrad felt like catching up with an old brother-in-arms. Because I chose to save Triss at Loc Muinne, the political landscape for mages felt earned, and my decision to forgive Letho led to one of the most rewarding "full circle" moments in the game. Seeing a character I could have easily killed in the last game actually standing before me, offering his help, added a layer of moral weight I haven't felt since my time with the most reactive RPGs.

One thing is for sure: I am finally ready to jump into [b]Cyberpunk 2077[/b] after this. The world-building CD Projekt Red achieved here is too rich to ignore, and I’m dying to see if they can replicate this level of narrative continuity in a new setting. My only hope is that the "choices matter" DNA remains intact. After seeing how they respected my loyalty to Roche and my mercy toward Letho, I want to see them push boundaries even further in a futuristic setting without losing that grounded, character-driven soul.

Gameplay-wise, the acting by Doug Cockle and the return of familiar faces is top-tier and sets a high bar. The combat feels like a fluid, high-budget evolution of the second game’s mechanics—the signs feel more integrated, and the alchemy system is more accessible without losing its depth. Some of the map markers in Skellige can feel a bit overwhelming compared to the focused hubs of [i]Witcher 2[/i], and the weight of the political jargon is still heavy, but the payoff of seeing your personal history reflected in the world is worth it.

TL;DR
🎮 [i]Witcher 2[/i] veterans will love seeing Roche and Letho return based on their choices.
🧠 A masterpiece of storytelling where "mercy" or "loyalty" from the past actually pays off.
🌍 The transition to a true open world makes the Northern Kingdoms feel massive yet personal.
⏳ Cyberpunk is next! If the consequences are even half as deep as this, I’m sold.

It is super unfortunate that this game is practically unplayable over a decade after its release. The game gets in the way of playing the game.

The graphics are gorgeous but there are so many issues still that affect basic game play. Glitching through walls and staircases, random bright spots where the screen washes out due to shader issues or whatever, issues with the display showing weird glitching or not being full screen despite being on a full screen setting, it needs an outside program (besides Steam) to launch, movement issues with the horse mount, there were just so many things that I kept running into that caused me to continuously  quit and restart the game. 

 The quick access menu has known issues that have been around since the game release and are yet unfixed. I cannot quickly access things like bombs or my torch and this instantly tanks gameplay. 

It looks great and for those that it runs well for, it has to be a super fun!
As it stands, for how the game was performing for me and considering how long it has been since its release, I would call this unplayable and would not recommend.

When people talk about The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt, it often gets treated as the natural peak of the series, but the truth is a bit more interesting than that. It doesn’t just improve on The Witcher and The Witcher 2: Assassins of Kings. It breaks away from them in design philosophy. Where the first game leaned heavily into slow, methodical RPG systems and the second focused on tight, politically driven storytelling with more contained areas, The Witcher 3 opens everything up. It trades some of that focused structure for scale, freedom, and a world that feels far less like a sequence of levels and far more like a living, breathing place.

That shift is exactly what makes it stand out, and also where some of its problems begin.

The most obvious difference is the open world. Unlike the previous games, which were segmented and more controlled in how they delivered content, The Witcher 3 drops you into massive regions and lets you find your own path. And to its credit, it handles that transition better than most RPGs. The world isn’t just big for the sake of it. It’s dense with stories, characters, and small moments that feel meaningful. Side quests in particular are where the game separates itself not only from its predecessors but from most of the genre. What could have been filler content is instead some of the strongest writing in the entire experience. You’re not just clearing objectives, you’re uncovering tragedies, making difficult choices, and dealing with consequences that don’t always feel fair or clean.

Playing as Geralt of Rivia also feels more refined here than in the earlier games. He’s still very much his own character, but the dialogue system and writing give you more room to shape how he responds to the world without losing his identity. The moral grayness the series is known for is still front and center, but The Witcher 3 presents it on a much larger scale. Entire questlines can hinge on decisions that don’t have obvious right answers, and the outcomes often take time to fully reveal themselves.

Where it really distances itself from the earlier entries is in how cinematic and accessible it feels. The presentation is smoother, the pacing is more approachable, and the overall experience is less punishing in terms of systems. That makes it easier for new players to jump in, but it also means some of the complexity and edge from the first two games gets softened. The earlier titles had a certain rough, almost experimental feel to their mechanics and storytelling. The Witcher 3 polishes that into something more mainstream, which is both a strength and a loss depending on what you valued in the series.

Combat is one of the clearest examples of this tradeoff. It’s more fluid and approachable than in the previous games, where it could feel clunky or overly technical. Here, it’s easier to pick up and generally more responsive, especially when mixing swordplay with signs and alchemy. But at the same time, it can lack depth. Once you find a rhythm that works, many encounters start to feel similar. Enemy variety helps, but not always enough to keep combat consistently engaging over such a long game. Compared to the tighter, more deliberate fights in The Witcher 2, this can feel like a step sideways rather than forward.

The open world, as impressive as it is, also brings its own set of issues. There’s a lot to do, but not all of it is meaningful. Points of interest can become repetitive, especially if you fall into the trap of trying to clear everything on the map. Travel can feel slow at times, and while the world is beautiful, there are stretches where you’re moving between objectives more than actively engaging with the game. Roach, your horse, has improved since earlier entries but still feels unreliable enough to be frustrating in tight situations.

The UI and inventory management are another area where the game shows its age. Navigating menus, sorting items, and managing gear can feel clunky and unintuitive, especially compared to more modern RPGs. It’s not game breaking, but it’s noticeable over time. There are also occasional pacing issues in the main story, where the sheer size of the world can dilute the urgency of what should feel like a focused narrative.

Despite all of that, what The Witcher 3 does well is hard to overstate. The writing is consistently excellent, the world feels alive in a way few games achieve, and the sheer amount of high quality content is remarkable. The expansions, especially Blood and Wine, don’t just add more, they refine and elevate the experience even further.

In the end, The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt stands apart from the first two games because it chooses scale and accessibility over tight structure and mechanical complexity. It’s a broader, more cinematic experience that sacrifices some focus in exchange for immersion and freedom. That decision is exactly why it became so widely praised, and also why some long time fans feel it lost a bit of what made the earlier games unique.

It’s not flawless, and it’s not a perfect evolution. But it is a bold one. And for most players, what it gains far outweighs what it leaves behind.

First of all, I'm wanting to play this game since the start of highschool days then finally with a big sale just last year came out I bought it(this game always discount though). Well surely it just been on my library for a while, and yeah I attempted to play it after I finished my university degree and graduation. This game also was the first on my RPG list that I wishlisted long time ago before I decide to test this game.

Let's start...This game really caught my interest because I tend to have a short span syndrome if I don't like the game maybe in term of gameplay of story after 1 hour until 3 hour. But this game was different it has a good slow-paced start from the beginning until the end of story.... I guess for a people who doesn't play the previous installement this game sure makes you confused at first. For me, yeah I basically don't know nothing about the lore of witcher, about Geralt, his origin and other stuff. I felt drawn to this game story, atmosphere, dark fantasy world, characters, and yeah Open World!. Anyway.. back to story, I really enjoyed the story from the beginning until the end because every choice you've made matter to the story, luckily because I read dialogues and use my feelings in dialoges I got a good ending on my first run😁, I nearly cried because I thought well I've got a bad ending on my first run...aaaa instead it's surprise that the good ending is really funny and heartwarming. That was good really good.

About character, well I do like the characters though even though I don't know their lore in the previous installement. Triss, Lambert, Yen, Letho, Vesemir, Ciri, Rocheand, Djikstra and Geralt's other allies. For me, of course our lone white wolf, Geralt..he's sometimes being funny sometimes being serious, well his dialogues choice sometimes kinda SuS and awkward to me and I thought it's okay so I got the general idea what's his personality looks like. His objectives was simple to be honest when being drawn out by a straight line, just to find his adopted daughter before being captured by strong enemy faction. That's it..period but He face may obstacle or like chores to do so it became a long mission to succeed, and I honestly like it. CDPR really made this story a true gem. I've played Cyberpunk first before this and well after comparing it, the two game are good, enjoyable, satisfying but I think if I rank it like personal ranking Witcher 3 is way higher than Cyberpunk. A total of 140 hours for my playtime(base game), it worth of your time. Take your time slowly and enjoy this game, to anyone who wants to play this game.

Overall, I can't rank lower than 10 so I gave this game at 10 out of 10. It's just really a damn good game for 2015 game. Even still clunky on some point, but hey that was 10 years ago. Played this game on Normal mode, still at some encounter it's hard for me because I'm a skill issue person... people said try DeathMarch difficulty if it's challenging for a casual for me or just want to make me suffer🤣. The next-gen quality update also damn good on my system with DX11, even I'm not using DX12 I can still use medium and Ultra on some settings with a little tweak. correction, DX12 on my system has image issues :)))

Final verdict, if you are interested in RPG even at slightest, heavy RPG like this.. I can say it's suits for you.

I beat this all the way back in 2018-2019 before the Next-Gen Update and I'm currently finishing playthrough with the next-gen update before the upcoming DLC for this game comes out. To this day I haven't played a game that comes close to this one in terms of story, gameplay, lore, and what have you. It's a near perfect story paired with awesome gameplay that is incredibly immersive. Still in my top 5 favorite game stories of all time. There's only a couple of quests that I had a lot of frustration with and I hope are somewhat retconned in the next game.

A real shame that the author will take inspiration from the games while not acknowledging the inspiration for some of the world-building the games bring considering how much of a disaster all of the Netflix-related media was. The one good thing that came out of that was the Next-Gen update quest I thoroughly enjoyed that and it shows the CD-Projekt Red still knows how to make great content.

The only complaint I have right now is that I wish that RED-Launcher wasn't forced at launch as I feel like it has caused a few stability issues with the occassional crash (I'm on Linux). I believe these issues were recently fixed but I still have other issues regarding playing with higher graphics settings though I suspect this to be the case with other games.

Despite my personal gripes with Cyberpunk 2077 and how that was initially handled, I do believe that the studio is back on track and I hope that the Witcher 4 will hold up as Ciri is deeply compelling character in this game.

TL;DR: Incredible story and one of the few games that actually justifies the time it takes to complete. A few technical issues for me due to the Next-Gen update and RED-Launcher.

To be honest, I didn’t really like The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt. It definitely had its moments and there’s a lot of cool stuff in it, but overall I didn’t get much joy out of playing it. It took me about 10 months to finish, which kind of says it all.

The combat is fun at first, but not very deep. You mostly end up relying on the same couple of abilities, and the rest feel pretty pointless. I liked the basic fighting and movement, but after a while it just got too easy. Even on the hardest difficulty, I didn’t find much challenge, especially later in the game.

The story is good, with some strong characters, but I really did not like Yennefer at all. She just didn’t land for me, and it actually made parts of the story less enjoyable. There are other characters I liked a lot more, which made that contrast even stronger.

The contracts are easily the best part of the game. Going around tracking and hunting monsters genuinely makes you feel like a witcher, and those moments were by far the most enjoyable for me. I really wish the game leaned more into that, because it’s where it shines the most.

Overall, it’s not a bad game, but I wouldn’t recommend it. It’s really long, and unless you have a lot of time, it might not be worth it. Honestly, I’d say just watch the show instead.

The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt — ★★★★☆ (4.5/5)
Combat & Gameplay
Geralt’s combat is solid, but it isn't the game's strongest suit. On the other hand, playing as Ciri is incredibly fun and fast-paced; it offers a much higher adrenaline rush. I honestly felt that Ciri was both more powerful and more enjoyable to play than Geralt.
Atmosphere & Music
The game features an incredible soundtrack and top-tier direction that truly immerses you in every scene.
Storytelling & Quests
For me, the best part of the game is the storytelling. The quests are brilliantly written, and the dialogue is excellent, giving every character significant depth. Even the side quests in the main story felt meaningful and engaging, rather than just "filler" content.
World & Choices
The world is beautiful and exploration is a joy; every region has a distinct atmosphere that pulls you deeper into the journey. Furthermore, choices have a real impact on events, which made me think carefully about every decision I made.
The Downsides
I did encounter some technical issues, such as occasional crashes during cutscenes, which broke the immersion a bit. Additionally, Geralt’s combat can feel somewhat repetitive over time, especially when compared to the excitement of playing as Ciri.

Jumping into this game 10 years after its release, I can say it still holds up for the most part. I hadn’t played the previous Witcher games, which made it a bit harder to fully grasp the story early on. For the first couple of hours, I was losing interest pretty quickly, but I decided to stick it out. The story started to grow on me and really picked up toward the second half of the game. I mostly stuck to the main questline, but I did notice what felt like hundreds of other quests available. This game has the potential to consume countless hours if you're interested. Being a game from 2015 with a next-gen update, it still looks decent. The biggest downside for me was the combat, which feels dated and was easily my least favorite part of the experience. Both DLCs were fine, with solid stories and some big plot twists at the end. Overall, I’d say this game is worth trying, but don’t expect it to be amazing. It goes on sale often, and picking it up for under $10 is a hard deal to beat.

If you’re reading this and wondering whether you should buy The Witcher 3, let me save you some time: don’t just buy The Witcher 3—buy The Witcher 3 with all of its DLCs. This is one of those rare games that completely ruins your expectations of what gaming should feel like afterward.

If it’s not the characters, story, or gameplay that wins you over, then it’ll be the world itself. The environments feel alive, the music is unforgettable, and the atmosphere has a depth that very few games ever achieve. Sometimes you won’t even be doing quests—you’ll just find yourself standing still, watching a sunset while the soundtrack plays in the background.

The UI is clean and intuitive, character progression feels rewarding, and by the time you’ve built Geralt properly, you can become almost unstoppable. But what truly sets this game apart is the quality of its stories. Even side quests often feel better written than the main plots of other games, and many of them will stay with you long after you finish.

If you’re hesitant because this is the third installment, don’t be. Yes, it continues Geralt’s story and includes references to earlier games, but you absolutely do not need to have played them to enjoy this one. The Witcher 3 stands perfectly well on its own.

For context, when I first played it, my only experience with larger RPGs was Skyrim and the Arkham series. The moment I started The Witcher 3, it dwarfed them in scale, depth, and immersion. It impressed me so much that I eventually bought all the other Witcher games and even the books.

After my first playthrough—which was only the base game, because I was also unsure about spending too much money on it—I remember finishing the game and feeling this strange sense of fullness, like I had experienced something so complete that the world around me suddenly felt empty for a while.

Out of all the games I own, this is the one I can honestly say I wish I had bought sooner.

I hope this helps someone make their decision. This game is almost 11 years old now, so I don’t know how many people are still on the fence—but if there’s anyone out there hesitating, this is for you, buy the game DLCs and all and enjoy.

Definitely won't deny that the game is slow-paced for the first hour or so, but once you get used to the controls its the lore that pulls you in deeper into the game. Additionally. grinding for upgrading your gear and strategically planning every move is a breath of fresh air instead of just jumping in a fight and hitting the enemy continuously until they die.

100% know now why this game got a GOTY back in the day, superb and still one of the benchmarks of a great game up to this day. 

Final Rating - 10/10 (might tone it down to 9.5 because Roach keeps getting stuck on weird places and for some reason doesn't know where to turn even if her life depended on it.)

I’m about 30+ hours in, and I feel like I’ve barely scratched the surface—in the best way possible. If you’re looking for a game where you can truly get lost, this is it.

The World & Exploration
The map is absolutely massive, but it doesn’t feel "empty" like some other open-world titles. Every village you walk into feels lived-in, and every notice board has something interesting. I’ve spent hours just riding Roach through the forests of Velen or sailing around Skellige, and the atmosphere is incredible. The dynamic weather and lighting still look stunning even by today's standards.

Gameplay & Quests
What really sets this game apart is the writing. Even the "minor" side quests often have better plots and more difficult moral choices than most games' main storylines. You’ll go in thinking you’re just hunting a monster for some coin, and two hours later, you’re caught in a deep political conspiracy or a tragic family drama.

The combat took me a second to click with, but once you start using Signs (magic) and Alchemy (oils and potions) properly, it becomes a really satisfying dance. Preparing for a big monster hunt by researching its weaknesses makes you actually feel like a professional Witcher.

A Few Thoughts:
Gwent: I am not entirely convinced by this mini-game, i dont see the attraction to it and its annoying when quest lines force you into it.

Roach: Has a mind of its own sometimes, but honestly, it just adds to the charm.

Choices: Be careful—this game remembers what you do. A "small" decision you make in hour 10 might come back to haunt (or help) you in hour 30.

Final Verdict: 33 hours in and I’m already certain this is one of the best RPGs I’ve ever played. If you haven't picked it up yet, just do it especially if its found on sale. Your social life can wait.

10 years. Hundreds of hours. And I still think about it.

The Witcher 3 is not just the best RPG ever made — it's one of the best pieces of interactive fiction ever created. A decade on, nothing has come close to dethroning it, and I'm not sure anything will.

What CD Projekt Red built here is staggering in scope but intimate in execution. The main story alone would be a masterpiece, but it's the side quests that truly set this game apart. Quests that in any other RPG would be throwaway fetch missions here carry genuine weight, moral ambiguity, and consequences that linger long after the credits roll. Bloody Baron. A Heart of Stone. The Isle of Mists. These are moments I'll carry with me forever.

Geralt of Rivia is one of gaming's greatest protagonists — not because he's the loudest or the most powerful, but because he feels real. Weathered, weary, and deeply human despite being anything but.

The world breathes. NPCs have routines, villages have histories, and every corner of the map rewards curiosity. Even now, replaying it, I find things I missed. The writing is sharp, the music is haunting, and the art direction is timeless.

If you've somehow never played this — stop everything. If you're considering a replay — yes, do it. The next-gen update only makes a masterpiece more beautiful.

This game is an absolute MARVEL. The atmosphere of this game is an experience like no other. The environment, the music, the setting, the landscape... it all coalesces together to immerse you into the universe, and dont get me started with the quests and writing in this game. GODS THE WRITING IS SO GOOD.

Most of the side quests in this game are never black or white. It will make you question your own morality with every choice you make. Choices WILL have consequences. No other game ive played has had the balls to make the player actually stop and think about the consequences of their actions so much as The Witcher 3 has. I honestly believe it shapes you as a person. It certainly moved me to the core. 

I can definitely see the combat be hit or miss depending on the person. It didnt hit for me at first either. The first time I played, I was pretty frustrated just due to the fact that theres a metric ton of stuff to keep track off (the wall of tutorials definitely didnt help the first time), but once it clicked, it clicked. I just want to suggest players should definitely play the game on a harder difficulty like Death March. The harder difficulties forces you to actually interact with the games systems, like Potions, or Oils, or Decoctions, or actually reading the Beastiary. It all adds to the immersion of being this badass Witcher that kills monsters for a living. In lower difficulties, it just doesnt feel the same, and it can ultimately get really boring quite fast. 

Overall an amazing experience that everyone should at least try to get into. This game is pure ART

Picked this up, played for a bit, then dropped it for almost a YEAR. It sat in my backlog collecting dust like every other “I’ll get back to it someday” game.

Then Hearts of Stone DLC happened.

Something just clicked. The writing, the pacing, the characters, SHANI, suddenly I wasn’t playing a game anymore, I was locked into a story I couldn’t put down. Next thing I know, I finished the base game and rolled straight into Blood and Wine like it was mandatory. No burnout, no breaks just pure immersion.

This game doesn’t try to hook you with flashy gameplay loops. In fact, combat can feel a bit clunky, movement isn’t always smooth, and yeah, it shows its age in some areas. If you’re here expecting tight combat mechanics, you might bounce off early, like I did.

But that’s not what The Witcher 3 is about.

Treat it like a NOVEL. Take your time, listen to the dialogue, get invested in the world and its characters. Once you shift that mindset, the game goes from “kinda slow” to “I can’t stop thinking about what happens next.”

Hearts of Stone is where it truly proves its writing mastery, and Blood and Wine feels like a perfect closing chapter.

Came back expecting to uninstall it again. Ended up finishing everything. What an experience.

Would absolutely recommend.

A game in many people's backlog, I'm sure - finally finished it and all the DLC and it deserves all the praise it gets:
- Rich world-building and genuinely feels very alive and dynamic. I was shocked how many interactions and changes I came across depending on my actions. Absolutely a selling point, for sure.
- Stellar performances and voice acting.
- Character interactions and relationships were a favourite part for me.
- DLC is amazing - stories are better than the main story imo, so well worth the time!
- Beautiful art direction and environments - very impressive for its age, but I understand it did have an enhanced patch released semi-recently.
- Stellar soundtrack
- Roach (person or horse) best boy.
- Geralt is a great protagonist, and his relationship with Ciri is incredible in an industry where genuine well-written father-daughter relationships are rare.
- Gameplay is great, not as tight or satisfying as some other, more combat focused games, but very fun and fun to engage with all the mechanics.

Only slight nitpick is the UI in the menus can be extremely hard to grasp, especially on controller, so it took a little while for me to get used to it and use it efficiently.

Also came across a few bugs/glitches in my playthrough, but nothing game-breaking and I was never locked out of progression.

"I’ve spent hundreds of hours in this game, and I’ve realized that being a Witcher is basically being the world’s most talented disaster. My typical Tuesday consists of chugging questionable alchemical brews until my veins turn black, fighting a legendary griffin while halfway drunk on Mahakaman spirit, and then heading to a brothel in Novigrad to forget that a forest spirit just tried to eat me.

The world is a beautiful, depressing masterpiece of Slavic folklore where the music makes you feel deep emotions right before a monster rips your head off. You’re essentially a mutated professional who heals with alcohol, survives on 'potions' that would kill a horse, and somehow still finds time to be the most charming guy in the room. If you want a game where you can live a life of monsters, magic, and very poor lifestyle choices, this is it. 10/10, would get intoxicated and hunt dragons again."

I’ve got close to 10 hours in *The Witcher 3* so far and honestly… I’m already hooked way harder than I expected.

This game just *pulls you in*. The world feels alive in a way that most games try (and fail) to achieve. You’re not just running from quest marker to quest marker — every little side story feels like it actually matters. I went in thinking I’d just do a quick contract or two, and suddenly I’m deep into some messed up village story wondering how it got this dark.

Combat took a bit to click at first, but once it does, it feels really satisfying. Mixing swordplay with signs and potions makes every fight feel like you actually have to think like a witcher, not just button mash your way through.

And the writing? Seriously good. Like, way better than most RPGs. Characters feel real, choices actually feel heavy, and nothing is just “good vs bad” — it’s always complicated.

Also, the atmosphere is insane. Riding through the world, random encounters, music kicking in at the right moment… it just hits different.

Only 10 hours in and I can already tell this is one of those games I’m going to sink a *lot* of time into.

10/10 so far. Absolutely worth it.

I have hundreds of hours on this game on xbox one, ps4, and ps5 with the next gen update. I haven't played much lately but I doubt I'll ever stop coming back to this masterpiece. As someone whose favorite genre overall is souls likes and who also enjoys hardcore action games, this doesn't scratch either itch, but the combat in The Witcher 3 is more than serviceable, being leaps and bounds better than anything from the elder scrolls or any turn based rpgs. Combat isn't the point of this game, but it can be both fun and challenging, depending on your build and difficulty setting, and it can absolutely be satisfying and contribute to the feeling of being a badass monster slayer. I don't really have anything bad to say about this game overall. The ending of the main story is slightly lackluster after how good everything leading up to it is, but both expansions more than make up for that. The Witcher 3 is not a perfect game, but it is about the closest thing to one that I've ever played.

One of the extremely rare open world games that, for me, makes it feel worthwhile to check out every point of interest, look under every unturned rock, and get excited to seek out every sidequest.

The gray morality and gritty realism of the world bleed through in such beautiful and often painful ways.

Is it absolutely, completely flawless? No, of course not. There is no perfect game. Not even a perfect game within its own genre. I wish there were fewer invisible walls at the edges of the world. I wish I could see even more of the abundantly beautiful sights in the hidden places amidst the dreary wretchedness of Velen or the awe-inspiring islands of Skellige.

No game is perfect, but The Witcher 3 comes a lot closer to being the perfect open world game than most games will ever have any shot at. This is a legendary title that needs no introduction, and I fully endorse it.

Amazing Story. Intersting Characters. Great side stories as well.

The combat can feel a bit janky sometimes, some quests might have a small bug here or there but overall this is such a great game with a lively world to explore. 

The story progression feels very natural with characters changing there opinions or life choices according to our interference. Every region feels different in there own way, there customs, problems and lifestyles like in real life. The world in witcher 3 looks and feels like it keeps moving forward rather than a still world for us to roam around. 

The quest sometimes feel like a repeat of the previous one, but most of the time it doesn't, which makes you want to complete as many quests as you are able to since a lot of the times the side quest evolves into one of the most heart felt questline you might have played in this game.

[h1] MASTERPIECE [/h1]
After spending 200+ hourse on this game, which is the most time I have ever spent on a game.  I can confidently say this is the best game I have ever played and very few games even come close. This isn’t just a game to me anymore, it’s an experience that completely changed how I look at gaming.

[h2] Graphics Quality [/h2]
For a game that was released in 2015, Witcher 3 still looks insanely good even today. The lighting, character models, landscapes, weather effects, etc. everything holds up beautifully. Whether you’re walking through villages or riding roach through green hills at sunset, the game constantly reminds you how much love and craftsmanship went into it.

[h2] Story Telling [/h2]
The storytelling is where Witcher 3 truly becomes legendary. The narrative is deep, intriguing, emotional, and full of drama. You’ll find romance, action, mystery, slice-of-life moments and unexpected plot twists woven together effortlessly.
And let’s be real you get choices:
- Be a full-on menace / ♥♥♥♥ boy
- Or stay loyal to your baddie

[b](And ofcourse… Yennefer over anyone. Always.)[/b]

Every quest feels meaningful. Even side quests have better writing than entire main stories in other games.

At first, I didn’t fully understand the story. I’m not a book reader, so I started by watching the Netflix Witcher series (up to Season 3 basically the Henry Cavill era). Honestly? I loved the show. Henry Cavill absolutely carried it and it was a solid entry point into the Witcher world.
This show made jumping into the game way more enjoyableand once I did, I was hooked.

[h2] World Exploration [/h2]
Exploration in The Witcher 3 is on another level. It’s incredibly easy to get sidetracked and ig that’s a good thing.
Each region feels unique and memorable:

White Orchard – a humble beginning
Velen – dark and depressing
Novigrad – dense and political
Skellige – pure Viking beauty
[spoiler] Toussaint – colorful [/spoiler]

And then there’s Kaer Morhen…
The first time you arrive there?
Peaceful. Emotional. Nostalgic.
The music, the atmosphere, the quiet, the family, everything about Kaer Morhen hits differently. It feels like home.

[h2] Choices That Actually Matter [/h2]
This is a real RPG. Your choices matter and the consequences might not show themselves immediately but when they do, they hit hard. Entire story outcomes, character fates and endings change based on how you play.
Honestly, in my opinion There’s no “right” way to play. eventhough ther're bad endings. Just be yourself, make decisions naturally and let the story unfold. That’s where the magic is.

[h2] DLCs [/h2]
Let me be loud for this:
[b] THE DLCS ARE AMAZING. [/b]
Both 'Hearts of Stone' and 'Blood and Wine' are absolute fire! These aren’t just add-ons; they’re basically full games in terms of quality, storytelling and content. Blood and Wine alone could be Game of the Year.

[h2] And the Cherry on Top: GWENT [/h2]
Last but definitely not least…
[b] GWENT. [/b]
Do.
Not.
Skip.
It.
What starts as a “side card game” becomes an obsession before you even realize it. Collecting cards, battling merchants and random NPCs, GWENT has no business being this addictive.

There are some stuff Which I didnt like in this game:
- The horse riding (Roach) is honestly annoying and clunky. It frustrated me enough that I eventually used a Roach mod, which felt almost necessary in the later half of the game.
- Poor Quest Management. While doing a quest or after completing a quest, sometimes results in the tracker automatically enabling the next main quest, rather than staying disabled. which gets annoying at some point. Again, Mods saved this feature as well.
- Most of Vendors  get annoying by unskippable baseless dialogues before opening their inventory, adding unnecessary time to selling/buying, especially when using them multiple times. Once again, Mods clutched up for this annoying feature.
... there are more bugs and other annoying features but do not hesitate to use mods for better experience.

So, finally this masterpice still gets a 10/10 from me and I hope those who play this game enjoys it!

The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt is, in my opinion, a game everyone should play at least once. It’s absolutely worth its price, and since it often goes on sale, it’s accessible even on a tight budget. The world is visually stunning, with vivid landscapes and picturesque towns that still look amazing today. The game is also quite long, and its expansions—Hearts of Stone and Blood and Wine—feel like full standalone experiences rather than simple add-ons.

The combat isn’t the most complex out there, but I personally find it very satisfying. It feels fluid and responsive, and taking down enemies never really gets old—especially when you approach fights like a proper Witcher, using potions, oils, and enemy weaknesses. One thing I really love is the soundtrack: it blends bard-like instruments with strong fantasy and medieval vibes, and at times it even reminds me of bands like Eluveitie. As for Geralt, I genuinely enjoy his personality—he’s confident, a bit smug or cocky in a charming way, often funny, but also serious when it matters.

The creatures are great both in design and mechanics, and the bestiary adds a lot with its detailed, almost encyclopedic lore. While I did skip some dialogue because it occasionally felt repetitive, the characters can still be very memorable depending on how much you engage with them. On top of that, the modding community has done an incredible job keeping the game fresh over the years. Overall, I highly recommend it to any RPG fan looking for something that has aged incredibly well.

The Witcher 3, base game, is simply amazing. The world feels vast yet most of it feels unique. Even side missions have engaging stories.

For me, the things that are great in the base game are: the graphics, the world, the main story, the side missions and the music.

Things that are mediocre are: the combat/enemies and the itemization. While mediocre, these do the job for people that are mainly interested in the story of Geralt.

Hearts of Stone, the first DLC, adds a great story to the base game. This DLC is mostly story and has very few side missions. I liked it. It also makes the boss fights more engaging than the base game.

Lastly, Blood and Wine is not as great. This DLC adds a whole new area to the base game, with a main story and side quests. The main story is good but, as opposed to the main game, the new area has a lot more quests that feel repetitive, making exploration less interesting. The boss fights are also engaging like in Hearts of Stone but the regular monsters are extremely annoying as they now have a lot of mechanics that have you chasing them around. I suggest avoiding a lot of the side content for this one unless you're the kind of person that plays a game several times in different difficulties.

There are games you play… and then there are worlds that quietly claim a part of your soul. The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt belongs firmly to the latter.
From the very first step into its war-torn, wind-whispering landscapes, you don’t feel like a player—you feel like a wanderer burdened with destiny. The game doesn’t rush you. It doesn’t beg for attention. Instead, it unfolds like an ancient epic, patient and dignified, as if it knows you will stay.
At its heart stands Geralt of Rivia—not a hero in shining armor, but something far more compelling: a tired, thoughtful man navigating a world that rarely rewards goodness. His journey is not about saving the world in the traditional sense; it is about choosing how to live in a broken one. And those choices… they linger. They haunt. They matter.
The storytelling is, quite simply, masterful. Even the smallest side quest feels like a short story penned with care—often morally complex, often bittersweet. You might begin a simple contract for coin and end up questioning your own sense of justice. Few games dare to blur the line between monster and man this beautifully.
And then there’s the world itself—vast, breathing, alive. The swamps of Velen feel heavy with despair, Novigrad pulses with intrigue and danger, Skellige sings with wild, stormy freedom. Every region carries its own soul, its own rhythm. You don’t just travel—you arrive.
Combat, too, is a dance—steel and silver, signs and strategy. It rewards preparation, patience, and understanding. You are not the strongest being in this world… and that’s exactly why victory feels earned.
What truly elevates this masterpiece, though, is its emotional depth. The relationships—with Yennefer, Ciri, Vesemir—are not shallow attachments but bonds forged through history, sacrifice, and quiet moments. There is love here, but not the simple kind. There is loss, but not without meaning.
It’s no surprise that CD Projekt Red created something that feels less like a product and more like a living, breathing piece of art. You can sense the devotion poured into every detail—the music that aches, the skies that burn at sunset, the dialogue that feels almost… human.
And when the journey finally ends, something strange happens. The silence that follows feels heavier than any battle. Because you realize—you didn’t just finish a game.
You lived another life.
And somewhere, in a quiet corner of your mind, Geralt is still walking… sword at his side, searching for something he can never quite name.

After finishing the main game and thoroughly doing every friend side quest, I do not recommend this game to anyone who actually wants to play a video game. Yes, I know, clunky combat, but that's not even what I mean. Long, drawn out dialogue with medial world implications; filler everywhere and I do mean everywhere. By the 2nd half of the main quest you'll be wondering why you just wasted over 30 hours doing things for other people that don't really matter in the end. Yes *some* of your choices matter I guess but it isn't stuff that you'll remember or really feel that invested in aside from a couple in the main story. 

Is it a good game? Hardly. Checklist world map exploration, a ton of quests that had me coming out thinking "why'd I bother?" Repetitive and formulaic melee combat, and cutscenes that never feel like they're going anywhere until... suddenly it does...? It's a very strangely paced adventure with characters that albeit are charming, don't do the heavy lifting the rest of the game really should.

All in all, ok-good story, jarring conclusion, bizarre pacing, a stunning and beautiful world with a few good moments of exploration, and gameplay that'll make you wanting something more off of what could have been. It is a good interactive movie adventure, and if that's what you want in a game, you'll find solace here.
In 2026 still a banger and better than most other big singleplayer RPGs from recent years. Haven't even finished the base game yet, so lots of value for the price of the complete edition!

There's not much positive to say about the game, a let down from the prequel, which although it had it's own problems, it also had something going for it.

Graphically, the game looks stunning, the only redeeming quality.

Talents - boring "X does more damage" 

Combat wise - possible the greatest offender.
There are mainly two attacks (fast / heavy) that do not differ much aside from whichever benefits more based on your talents. You can also parry, which at best guarantees a single melee hit, that is assuming its not the type of attack that you can't parry, which will result in you getting stunned.
You can also either side step or roll, except there's no real penalty for side stepping so you can spam side step without a care. 
The signs are pretty pointless aside from protective shield and fire the rest are different colored "you are supposed to use this against that".  
Most of the combat devolves into enemies attacks -> sidestep -> 2 hit and repeat if group cast fire else cast shield (or anything as it doesn't matter), you could do something else but why would you all of the other options are just a lot less effectively and aren't even fun? I tried to make parrying more fun but it just seems pointless you take on risk (especially because of the unparryable attacks) for a single lame hit, even with a talent that makes parrying stronger, which you only get deep into the game. 

Game system wise - there are effectively none
Quests don't matter as they barely give any XP or gold, you'd have to grind 10s of quests for a single level up (besides, they heavily lean towards quantity over quality).
Material/gear rewards from doing anything are useless, as the best gear (it seems) is craftable from hard to miss treasure hunting. 
Gold doesn't matter because of aforementioned, that is unless you want to upgrade the witcher gear, which there is no reason to, as the game is very easy even on hardest difficulty.
Just using swords from the story and or witcher gear you end up 2-3 shotting all mobs.
Potions - maybe at the end of the game otherwise pointless (aside from nightvision). 
Bombs? pointless.
Crossbow? water gimmick and shooting down a specific mob (which you don't have to). 
Enemies - you will fight the same few mob types for the most part, no real bosses [spoiler] besides the ending 3 fights, unless you want to count "fighting yet another golem but with explosions nearby" tier fights as """boss fights""" [/spoiler].
There are a few other systems but they are all so meaningless I barely remember they exist.

Story wise - it's alright? maybe? The story was nice until about Novigrad?, which is probably the peak of the game in general. Afterwards it just heavily draws from characters and events that I can only assume are from the novel as they do not appear in the prequel, or just fluff that doesn't matter. The closer to the end the more the story relies on said characters and events making it very confusing and pointless to read.

The game also seems to crash semi frequently - once every 3 hours or so, and has quite a few bugs, especially wrt anything to do with water/swimming and boats.

Side note: I wouldn't call it a spoiler but just for the sake of it  [spoiler] seems odd they let every character show off skin yet had to cover Ciri up during a sauna scene. I understand the reasoning just seems silly. [/spoiler]

[b]The Hook:[/b] I spent 200 hours ignoring my missing daughter and the literal end of the world because a man in a muddy field told me his prize-winning goat was missing.

[h1]The Mutant Janitor[/h1]

[b]The Vibe:[/b] Geralt isn't a hero; he's a high-functioning pest control agent in a world that smells like wet dog and political betrayal. You save a village from a soul-eating forest demon, and your reward is 10 gold pieces and a stale piece of bread from a man who immediately spits at your feet. It’s the first game that perfectly captures the feeling of having a 9-to-5 job where the "office" is a swamp full of drowners.

[h1]The Rooftop Paradox[/h1]

[b]The Physics:[/b] Your horse, Roach, does not obey the laws of physics; she obeys the laws of eldritch geometry. One minute you’re galloping through a forest, and the next, your horse is performing a vertical handstand on the roof of a cottage because you looked at a fence the wrong way. Geralt himself moves with the grace of a cargo ship in a bathtub—he doesn't "walk," he just negotiates his weight with the earth.

[hr][/hr]

[b]Verdict:[/b]
09/10 as a narrative masterpiece.
10/10 as a card-collecting addiction disguised as a fantasy epic.

[i]If this review made you hear the "Lelelele" combat music in your kitchen, leave an award so I can pay off my Gwent gambling debts.[/i]

[b]Monsters, Politics & Consequence-Sprawled Open World Systems – The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt[/b]

[i]A vast open-world action RPG where monster hunting, branching narrative choices, alchemy preparation, and layered quest design combine into one of the most systemically rich fantasy experiences ever made. [/i]

[b]Core Gameplay & Open World Structure[/b]  
The Witcher 3 is built around a massive, seamless open world divided into distinct regions, each packed with quests, monster contracts, and narrative-driven encounters. You play as Geralt, a professional monster hunter navigating a war-torn continent where political factions, supernatural threats, and personal stories constantly overlap. Exploration is non-linear, with almost every activity feeding into world-building or character development. 

[b]Combat & Mechanical Flow[/b]  
Combat is real-time and stamina-based, blending swordplay, dodging, signs (magic), and item usage. Encounters reward preparation and positioning over pure reflexes — reading enemy types, applying oils, and choosing the right signs often matters as much as mechanical execution. Combat scales from grounded duels to chaotic multi-enemy fights where spacing and timing become critical.

[b]Alchemy, Crafting & Preparation Systems[/b]  
Preparation is a core pillar. Geralt uses alchemy to brew potions, oils, and bombs that significantly alter combat performance. Many of these tools are situationally essential, reinforcing a gameplay loop where knowledge of enemies directly translates into mechanical advantage. Preparation before fights often matters more than execution during them.

[b]Quest Design & Narrative Systems[/b]  
Quests are the game’s strongest mechanical system. Instead of simple objectives, they often branch into multi-layered investigations, moral dilemmas, and long-form consequences. Even side quests frequently contain fully developed story arcs, with decisions that can affect entire regions, characters, and future encounters.

[b]Choice & Consequence Design[/b]  
Choices are rarely binary good/evil. Instead, decisions exist in moral grey zones where outcomes are unpredictable or delayed. Many consequences only surface hours later, reinforcing a sense of long-term narrative weight rather than immediate feedback loops.

[b]Exploration & World Reactivity[/b]  
The world reacts dynamically to player actions through shifting NPC states, faction control, and evolving questlines. Environmental storytelling is dense, with hidden encounters and emergent moments scattered throughout exploration paths.

[b]Progression & Build Customisation[/b]  
Character progression is structured around combat skills, signs, and alchemy specialisations. Builds allow for hybrid playstyles — from aggressive sword-focused combat to sign-heavy magic builds or potion-enhanced survivability setups.

[b]Pacing & Mechanical Identity[/b]  
The pacing alternates between slow investigation, dense narrative interaction, and high-intensity combat encounters. The mechanical identity is defined by preparation, consequence, and systemic interconnection between quests, combat, and exploration.

[b]Verdict – 10/10[/b]  
The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt is a deeply systemic open-world RPG where combat, alchemy, and branching narrative design combine into a sprawling, reactive fantasy experience built around consequence and player-driven storytelling.

I had never played a witcher game or read one of the books before this one but after being completely confused for a few hours it hooked me, it's a very interesting world based on Scandinavian mythology that barely gets explored in media backed up by competent writing and solid game mechanics. After completing the game I bought the first two games on gog and have gone on to complete those as well in reverse order (Mainly because the first game is kind of manure ngl). 

The story is okay but I liked the fact that it’s heavily choice-dependant, there are a few decisions in the game that affect the ending of certain quests, the ending or even how characters perceive you when you encounter them later on. The world isn’t completely black and white, trying to be a hero in every situation may get you nowhere if all the people related to the quest die and you’re left without a reward, every time you make a decision you must be a pragmatist rather than a paragon even if it means engaging in corruption or wronging someone that may not deserve it.

The point that completely grabbed me about this game was the monster hunting, every monster you encounter that’s bigger than a wolf requires for you to investigate crime scenes to determine which monster you’re up against, based on that you have to prepare (Sometimes heavily) for the battle buying or crafting potions and materials based on the info you have on your log and the information provided by the quest, then if you prepared correctly the monster can go down in 5 minutes, 2 hours or completely demolish you in 30 seconds, all for enough silver to buy a sandwich it’s great!

I have mixed feelings on the DLC, the first one is very short with a single questline but it's very, VERY good. Meanwhile the second one is very long with a whole new map to explore but I found it to be bland, I think the developers were trying to make it fun as at least two quests are obvious jokes that lack in the main game but if that was the intent they fell rather flat on their face. So that's what I think you either get a very deep puddle or an ocean with the depth of a puddle; regardless I think the DLC is a good addition that with at least another 10-20 hours of gameplay and adds a little nice epilogue to the main game.

On negatives the first thing that I can think of is Gwent (A card game in the game). Gwent can go ♥♥♥♥ itself and so does the person that added it to the game, I hated whenever I had to do a mandatory match of it and lost all rounds on purpose because I wanted to get it done as soon as possible, I don’t care if an npc thinks less of me down the road because I didn’t feel like playing medieval Yu-Gi-Oh.

It’s a good experience and I highly recommend it even if you’ve never played another game or read one of the books.

*The Witcher 3* is an incredible game. The opening can feel a little slow and repetitive, with quests that initially come across like minor chores. However, as you progress, you realise many of those early tasks provide important context and information for your main objective: finding Ciri before the Wild Hunt.

Visually, the game still holds up extremely well. The graphics look especially impressive when you use Reshade to enhance the HDR presentation and overall image depth.

Combat is a clear improvement over *The Witcher 2*, with better flow, stronger abilities, and more satisfying mechanics overall.

I would highly recommend it to anyone who enjoys story-rich action-adventure games.

Spent around 50 hours on here and 30 on PS5 to beat just the main story, which is all my review is... Game play is NOT dated as some may say, as I highly enjoyed the swordplay and signs. Geralt is a true badass when time slows down and the dismembering of monsters occurs. The game visually has aged well in my opinion. I played with no mods or enhancements. The scenery and foliage still look breath taking to me. The facial animation on the characters do show age though. Story wise, I mean its amazing. That is the only word I can label it with. It has every fantasy fan's cravings in it. From monsters to black smiths. Side quests are always a treat and Geralt's commentary makes it worth it with his smart ass but humble remarks. Truly a one of a kind character and I cant see where they take this next.
