SO GOOD!  I can't believe I didn't play this sooner.  I played Cyberpunk 2077 right after I got my gaming PC, and thought I should check out CDPR's other titles.  When I looked at Witcher 3 though, I didn't like the theme / style / genre as much, and skipped it.  4 years later and Witcher 3 was on sale for a few bucks.  I thought I would give it a try and compare, and see why it had such a high metacritic score and so many people who call it one of the greatest.

The hype was real!  Even though I am not too into this type of game, I got SUPER into Witcher 3 and couldn't stop playing it.  Beat (all main / secondary quests) in the base game, Hearts of Stone, and Blood and Wine.  

The combat is ok (not great).  Graphics are ok (which is amazing considering it is 2026, 10+ years after it was released).  But the story and characters are soooo good.  Really hits.  Loved Geralt, Ciri, even stupid Roach, Yen, vamps, etc.  The story is pretty linear with only a few ways to alter the ending.  Lots to explore / do.

There is a lot to learn at the start, and it is overwhelming, but worth it.

I enjoyed gwent and got into it.  Played and won all the tournaments (highest score was 300+).

Horse riding and Roach were my main gripe.  They were super annoying at times.

Lots of fun mods out there that are worth checking out.  They can really help tweak the game and make it a perfect fit for you.

In the end, I think I liked this more than Cyberpunk 2077.  Witcher 3 is a 10/10 masterpiece.

Great RPG

Strong Suits:

Main Story (10/10): The games strongest point is definitely the story. It's about 50 hours long for the Base Game, 6-8 hours long in Hearts of Stone, and 10 hours long in Blood and Wine. The Villains are definitely interesting, with the strong points being Geralt and the Side Characters, as well as the many options the game gives you. From what I can assume, there is like 50 different ways the story can go. Definitely one of the best stories I've played.

Side Content (10/10): Another one of the game's best areas, is the side content. Most of the game's characters have associated side quests, which helps develop the character and can also influence the main story and the world around you. I've played only a few games with side content that actually matters, or influences anything, and this is one of them.

Open World (12/10): Probably the best Open-World, or one of the best Open-Worlds out there. Each area is unique and different, with even Blood & Wine adding an entire new area, and I'm pretty sure one of the most sizeable ones. It's definitely easy to get lost in certain parts of the map and explore, and the 3 Cities (Novigrad, Oxenfurt, and Beauclair) are all very dense, but well crafted.

Visuals & Atmosphere (10/10): The Visuals in this game are something else for being made in 2015. The Graphics are amazing, and you can quite easily run 120 FPS if you have a decent-good PC. Each and every area looks and feels different, with the Sound Track & SFX being amazing. Definitely one of the most visually astonishing games on the market, especially for when it was made.

Minor Flaws:

Quality of Life (7/10): The game has a lot of great quality of life features, but there are something issues I do have with the games quality of life, mostly during the later game. I enjoy the games fast travel system, and I also love the fact that you can store loot and items you find around the world in chests. My main issue with the games quality of life though lies after you beat the game. Once you finish the game, almost ALL of the games main side characters completely disappear, no dialogues, no shops, no nothing, and in my opinion, that is kind of a bummer, since I wish I could go back and talk to some of the side characters after beating the main story, this also applies to both DLCs, with the only talkable character in Blood and Wine being Anna, if you do get the good ending.

Combat (6/10): The Combat is probably the weakest part of the Witcher 3, at least in my opinion. For context, I played this game on KBM (Keyboard & Mouse) and not Controller, so it could differ between the two. The main weakness of the combat for me was functionality. It was incredibly clunky, and the animations were weird, as well as fighting multiple enemies being clunky. The base combat is also overly basic, it's just dodge hit dodge hit dodge hit, over and over again, in a very basic manner. The strengths of the combat though come from the Alchemy portion. The Alchemy in this game is amazing, there's almost a hundred potions if not more, with each and every one serving a purpose, and allowing you to give yourself advantages against certain enemy types. The spells in this game are also unique, allowing you to utilize them for different purposes. So all in all, it's not the worst combat system, but it definitely could use improvements.

Bosses (6.3/10): This is probably the hardest one for me to score, since some of the Boss Fights are great, and some aren't so great. The early-mid game boss fights are pretty decent, but quite boring, but the late game boss fights are not great. A lot of the Wild Hunt boss fights are incredibly repetitive, they spam the same 3 attacks over and over again, and are quite easy. I simply just wish they had more variety in their attacks, or weren't so annoying to fight, but nonetheless, they are fairly easy and should take less than 5 tries. The final boss fight was okay ish, he was very tanky so it took awhile to kill him, but he was definitely the most fun. With the DLCs, Blood & Wine by far has the best boss fights. I enjoyed almost all the boss fights in that DLC, with most of them having the most engaging move sets, while having great lore attached to them. Hearts of Stone though, not so much. Most of the boss fights were incredibly annoying, usually either having an ability that allowed them to heal themselves, or create like 20 clones of themselves. Nonetheless, this game has some bad boss fights, but also some great boss fights.

Overall Score: 9.3/10

The Witcher 3 is the best role playing game i have ever had the pleasure of playing. Yes it frequently crashes on PC and yes the combat does feel a little dated by todays standards however, there is no doubting that this game is truly something special and you will quickly forgive its quirks for the amazing and concentrated story. It immediately hooks you in and kept me invested throughout my 100+ hours playthrough. The characters are well thought out and each have a unique role. The worlds to explore are abundant and packed with optional side quests, treasure hunts, Witcher contracts and places to explore. The music is also excellent and matches the tone of the game very well, perfectly fitting with the current situation you are in. There is a lot to learn in The Witcher 3 but mastering even just the basics will reward you in your journey. The gameplay including combat is showing its age but is still perfectly acceptable and fun. I especially enjoyed playing through the Blood and Wine DLC with the vibrant and rich world of Toussaint drawing me deeper into the world of The Witcher 3. I highly recommend playing the Witcher 3 even in 2026.

its a good game but definitely a bit overrated 

Pros: 

Story - S Tier 
Characters - S tier
Quests - S tier 

+ World building and Huge map are also great. 
+ choice-based dialogue options 
+ gwent is a very fun card game . collecting the cards and the amount of heroes to choose from is also a plus. 

Cons:

Loot - There is a lot of it, but not a lot of variety. Mostly just swords or the occasional axe but those are really the only 2 options of melee combat. Most of the loot you are gonna find is gonna be worthless or you are going to out-level it. So yes lots of loot to find, but no not a lot of it to actually use and the variety is lacking. Swords arent really unique they will just have slightly better stats.. so getting a new legendary sword isnt really exciting. 

Items/Equipment - The crafting, alchemy, potions, bombs, range combat equipment are all very lacking. I played much of the game without really need to find ingredients, make potions, find and use runestones, upgrade your bombs etc. There are tons of potions but they dont really do enough to feel needed.  Yes you could play on a harder difficulty but still bombs and potions arent NEARLY as important as just getting good at timing parries and dodges. 
They didnt really do a good job at making all of this extra stuff in the game feel needed. 


traversal - Traversing map the world sucksss.  
your horse will not move if you touch any tiny tree or bush, so traveling on horseback is often a pain of roach halting every 10 seconds.  
Your horse and Geralt will not go up any incline that is like more than 30 degrees. its hard to tell what hills you can go over or not. 
and with tons of large mountains  in much of the map that you cant traverse. getting around is often more of a chore than enjoyment. 
Sliding down tiny hills, mantiling, geralt's jumping are all very janky and very much a pain. 

you cannot fast travel unless you are at specific sign posts making the awful traversing on horseback a requirement. 
all in all traversing the world is comically bad in this otherwise great game. 

combat/gameplay -  likely the most dissapointing aspect of the witcher. The combat is very boring. 
To start crossbows and bombs (range combat) are very weak and they are also auto-targeting , which feels pretty dissapointing to use. 

The melee combat is very boring. its just dodge + spam attack , dodge + spam attack. its a very boring and un-engaging combat loop.  if not for the other aspects of the witcher being S-tier,   witcher would be a very mid RGP with how boring the combat is...  but you just cant knock the other things that witcher just nails. 

theres ton of quests in this game. and tons of the quests are fun and not just tedious side-quests most rpgs fall into. 
The characters are great. the story is great.
The choice based aspect is also a highlight, you get to choose how you approach situations and which dialogue options  which is always a huge plus in RPGs.

Owning this game on PS4, PS5, Nintendo and now on PC feels criminal, but I just can't get over this game. I've finished it not once, not twice, but 7 times and I still sometimes don't get the references my friends  tells me. 

If you are the book lover, don't expect this to be fully accurate, the characters look different (some of them at least - e.g. - Geralt was not that hot in the books, get it? The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt is not just a game — it’s an unforgettable journey. From the moment you step into the world as Geralt, the game immerses you in one of the most detailed and alive open worlds ever created. Every village, forest, city, and battlefield feels handcrafted with incredible care and atmosphere.

The storytelling is absolutely phenomenal. Even side quests have better writing and emotional depth than most full games. Your choices genuinely matter, and many decisions stay with you long after you finish the quest. The characters are memorable, realistic, and full of personality, especially Geralt, Ciri, Yennefer, and Triss.

Combat becomes extremely satisfying once you master signs, potions, and swordplay, while monster hunting feels rewarding and immersive. The soundtrack is masterpiece-level and perfectly captures both the beauty and darkness of the world.

What truly makes this game special is the amount of passion and content packed into it. The expansions, especially Blood and Wine, could easily be full standalone games because of their quality and size.

Even years after release, this game remains one of the greatest RPGs ever made. If you enjoy rich storytelling, meaningful exploration, and a world that feels alive, this is a must-play masterpiece.

I’ve completed The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt three times, and it’s still one of my favorite games ever—and easily my favorite in the Witcher series.

What makes it special for me is the atmosphere. The world feels deeply rooted in Slavic folklore, and that gives it a unique, almost authentic soul. The music is powerful and emotional, inspired by traditional folk sounds, that make every moment feel alive.

The quests deserve a special mention. Even “simple” side quests can turn into unexpected moral dilemmas. Sometimes there’s no good choice, only consequences—and that’s what makes it so memorable.

Also, Geralt and Yennefer? One of my favorite love stories in any game. It’s not perfect or sweet—it’s complicated, intense, and feels real. The chemistry, the arguments, the history between them—you can feel that these two have been through a lot together. (Yes, I always choose Yennefer. No regrets.)

Even after three playthroughs, I still discover new details, new dialogues, new outcomes.

This game doesn’t just tell you a story—it pulls you into its world and doesn’t let go.

Over 260 hours on record, and I still get the urge to reinstall it just to play a few hands of Gwent. Let’s be real, The Witcher 3 doesn't just raise the bar for RPGs; it completely breaks it and ruins other games for you.

The most insane thing about this game is the writing. You’ll pick up a random, seemingly boring side quest to find an old lady's missing frying pan, and somehow it spirals into a deeply emotional story involving espionage and war crimes. The world feels incredibly lived-in, dark, and unforgiving. Your choices actually matter and carry a heavy weight, often forcing you to choose between the lesser of two evils where nobody really gets a happy ending.

And then there's the immersion. Riding through Velen while the trees sway in the wind, with that hauntingly beautiful soundtrack playing in the background, is an experience you don't forget. And the DLCs? Hearts of Stone has one of the best villains in gaming history, and Blood and Wine is basically an entire sequel disguised as an expansion.

Is it 100% flawless? Nah. The combat can feel a bit floaty and repetitive once you figure out your build, swimming controls like you're steering a broken fridge, and Roach will absolutely spawn on a roof or get stuck on a tiny fence when you need her most.

But honestly, none of that matters. The sheer scale, the storytelling, and the atmosphere make those minor annoyances irrelevant. If you haven't played this yet, do yourself a favor: buy it, get the expansions, say goodbye to your social life for the next couple of months, and remember... always accept a match of Gwent.

Fantasy as a genre is something I find myself drawn to a lot of the time but it's also very hard to get right. Just having a world of swords, shields, magic, elves, and dragons isn't enough for you to get it right. More often than not it leaves me experiences a lot of fantasy worlds but never truly falling in love with them. There is just so much that Witcher 3 gets right that I don't even know if you can list everything. However, the actual world itself in my opinion is what sells this universe. The environments feel endless and all of these areas are filled to the brim with monsters and magical oddities that fit the gritty vibe the Witcher is known for. Cities are densely packed and feel very real unlike most other games where the cities feel like a doll house.

At this point I think most people know that the story is incredible. The way that your choices genuinely change character storylines and the world as a whole makes you feel apart of it. You get to see nuanced characters deal with this world that is at war with a complexity and realism that builds up the world and story. 

The combat itself isn't too impressive which is fine to me. If anything the combat gets more engaging the higher difficulty you choose because it forces you to actually delve deep into mechanics that you will never need to on the easiest difficulties. If you're willing to look past that it is a must-play for anyone who enjoys RPGs.

Re-playing on a higher spec PC after a few years, with the free update for the "next gen" visuals, the game looks stunning and the story is a compelling and fun as ever. 

Overall-
Story- 7.5/10- voice acting is top notch and characters are unique throughout. Some side quests do become repetitive and there are some similarities between them that could have been lightly altered to add to the immersion a bit. The main story quests are mainly well structured and do a good job of introducing new features and mechanics, but some of the grind becomes annoying at harder difficulties. I played at the second hardest difficulty, which I feel made it a bit harder to focus on the story, as I was constantly crafting and optimizing weapons for encounters rather than 100% focusing on the story. Had I played at a lower difficulty, I can see myself giving a higher rating for the story.

Combat - 7/10 - Some of the lower aspect of the score might be a result of having played more recent games that do some of the combat aspects more justice. For example, the sword play is fun and there is a large range of different mechanics and crafting materials you can use to enhance your weapons and defenses, but using them in combat is a bit clunky at times, and overall many of the enemy moves and attacks become easily judged after some time. Compared to a game like Elden Ring or DS games for example, the harder enemies and bosses are not nearly as challenging. They definitely pose a challenge at harder game difficulties, but their moves and use of the environment is a bit limited for such an open world game. 

Additionally, mounted combat leaves a lot to be desired and feels particularly unpolished compared to other games with mounted combat, like Elden Ring, Crimson Desert, or even RDR2 (even though that combat is usually ranged). Overall, the horse movement across the world does break immersion a bit, and 99% of the time I dismount to fight enemies if they are mounted, since their movement is predictable and slow. 

Sword combat is fun and feels fresh across the first playthrough. I wish it was a bit harder to light/heavy attack spam most enemies, and the i-frames around finishing moves back it pretty easy to string kills together. This makes the game fun and cinematic at times, which is great ( think like original Assassin's Creed games like II and Revelations, where chaining kills becomes easy as you level up), but also makes fighting most enemies that litter the environment more of a nuisance at times than an actual challenge. 

World/Environment - 9/10 - Easily one of the better constructed open world games I have played. There are very few times where the world feels empty or unpopulated. On Ultra details, there is simply never a dull area, be it a battlefield littered with bodies, weapons, and rotting animals, or a small fishing village with shops, smiths, and homes. The world feels alive, NPCs react to your character and your behavior in a pretty realistic way for the time this game came out, and stumbling upon side quests or civilians in distress to rescue actually feels organic and derived from exploration, not just following pings or map markers.

Graphics/Performance/Bugs - 8/10 - The game looks stunning for a 10+ year old title. On my system, the game runs flawlessly at 1440p, FPS never dips to a point where it is at all noticeable to me, on all settings maxed out other than not running 4K resolution. There is definitely some noticeable pop-in at long distances, and some terrain fill and meshing becomes a little visible if you rapidly sweep the camera or enter a new area, but it's never really immersion breaking. 

As for bugs, I would say it was a bit disappointing to still encounter map geometry breakage, T-posing character at times, and buggy water/swimming interactions during one early mission in a cave that led to having to reload the game from a prior save to reset. For a game that has received this much support and updates, given I encountered many of these bugs in the first 10 hours of the game, I would hope they would have been addressed by now. However, they are more of an annoyance than anything else, and won't affect 99% of your actual playthrough in my experience. 

Overall  - 8/10 - Given the game is frequently on sale and already discounted, I think this is a no brainer. Anyone looking to jump into The Witcher 4 soon who has not played 3, should definitely give it a playthrough if you can.

The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt - A Timeless Journey Through a Living World

Even after completing the game multiple times, including New Game Plus runs, The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt continues to stand as one of the most immersive RPG experiences ever created.

Story - 8.5/10
The narrative is rich, layered, and emotionally engaging. It doesn’t just tell a story, it pulls you into a morally complex world where choices matter, often without clear right or wrong answers. While not flawless, its depth and character development make it unforgettable.

Graphics - 7/10 (Original) | 8.5/10 (Next-Gen Update)
For its original release, the visuals were impressive, though not groundbreaking by today’s standards. However, the next-gen update breathes new life into the game, significantly enhancing lighting, textures, and overall atmosphere, bringing it closer to modern visual expectations.

Combat - Accessible but Less Challenging
Compared to Souls-like games, the combat is more forgiving and approachable. It lacks the punishing difficulty some players seek, but it compensates with fluid mechanics, variety in builds, and the satisfying use of signs, potions, and swordplay.

Exploration & World Design - Exceptional
This is where the game truly shines. The world is vast, detailed, and alive. Every region feels distinct, filled with meaningful side quests, hidden stories, and environmental storytelling. It’s not just big, it’s purposeful.

Roach Mechanics - A Minor Flaw
Traversal is generally smooth, but controlling Roach can sometimes feel clunky, especially when compared to more refined mounts like Torrent (Elden Ring). It’s a small issue, but noticeable during exploration.

Comparison to Previous Titles
The Witcher 3 offers significantly more depth, scale, and freedom compared to its predecessor. It expands on everything - storytelling, world-building, and gameplay systems, making it a definitive upgrade.

Final Verdict - 8/10
A vast, beautiful, and deeply engaging world that continues to hold its ground years after release. While it has minor flaws, the overall experience is something few games have matched.

Pro:

A richly detailed open world that feels alive, with meaningful side quests that often rival the main story in quality

Strong narrative writing with memorable characters and emotionally impactful choices that carry real weight

Excellent expansions that add substantial content and some of the best storytelling in the entire game

Deep combat and progression systems that allow for different playstyles, especially when using signs, alchemy, and gear builds

Atmospheric soundtrack and environmental design that enhance immersion throughout the journey

---

Con:

Frequent crashes that can disrupt gameplay and lead to frustration, especially during longer sessions

Jittery hair and object physics that occasionally break immersion and make certain scenes feel less polished

Combat can feel clunky at times, particularly in tighter spaces or when dealing with multiple enemies

Movement and controls may feel slightly stiff, especially when navigating indoors

---

Rating: 9/10

The Witcher 3 remains one of the most impressive RPGs ever made, delivering an unforgettable world and story that set a high standard for the genre. While technical issues like crashes and visual quirks can pull you out of the experience, the overall depth, writing, and atmosphere more than make up for these shortcomings.

I’ve always struggled to find the right words to describe how I feel while playing, but The Witcher 3 deserves the effort. This isn’t just another open-world game, its an experience that completely absorbs you.
What impressed me the most was the richness of the dialogue. Unlike other games where you just skip through conversations, here I wanted to hear every word. Each interaction feels real, and while the missions follow a linear structure that keeps you focused, they are so well written that even the smallest task feels important to the world.

The level of detail is insane. You can lose track of time just taking in the scenery or watching how the villages react to the weather and the monsters. The world feels alive, organic, and dangerous.
The soundtrack is perfect, without exaggeration, it’s one of the best I’ve ever heard. The music sets the tone for every moment, from the tension of an epic combat to the melancholy of exploring an isolated forest. You can't play this without the music.

This is the kind of game where you jump in "just for half an hour" and suddenly five hours have passed. The way the story hooks you and how your decisions shape the fate of the characters is something few games manage to do this well.
If you’re looking for a game with soul, tons of content, and a story that stays with you long after the credits roll, don’t think twice. It’s worth every cent and every hour invested.

Score: 10/10

If your soul is tired of endlessly swiping through shallow profiles, ✨ The Witcher 3 is the long-term relationship you’ve been looking for. It’s a massive, beautiful world where every "match" has about fifty hours of emotional baggage and a side quest that will probably make you cry. 🐺🩹

The core gameplay is the ultimate romantic fantasy: you play as a professional "fixer" who looks great in leather and spends his time tracking down a lost loved one while getting distracted by literally everyone else's problems. It’s a loop of "ride a horse, talk to a ghost, play a card game for three hours, and occasionally fight a griffin." 🃏⚔️ It’s the only game where you can be in the middle of a world-ending crisis and still stop to ask a merchant for a round of Gwent—which is honestly how most of us handle our real-life problems anyway.

A must-play for anyone who wants a dating experience where the monsters are literal, the choices actually matter, and your horse is the only one who truly understands you. 🐎❤️

Play this if you enjoy:

Having a "type," and that type is specifically silver-haired mutants 🗡️

Spending more time on your card game addiction than the actual plot 🃏

Realizing that "choosing the lesser evil" is basically every Friday night out 🍻

A story so good it makes your real-life social circle look like a bunch of NPCs 👤

I can’t say enough good things about . This game completely pulled me in—not just because of the main story, but because of how rich and alive the entire world feels.

What really stood out to me were the stories and side quests. They’re not filler—they feel meaningful, emotional, and often hit harder than the main plot in other games. Every contract, every village, every character has depth, and you actually care about the outcomes of your choices.

The monster lore is on another level. I especially loved how the game leans into dark folklore—twisted, unsettling, and rooted in real-world legends. The way creatures like witches are portrayed—terrifying, predatory, and deeply disturbing—adds a layer of horror that makes the world feel dangerous and authentic. It’s not just fantasy; it feels like stepping into the darker corners of old myths and stories.

That horror element is what elevates the experience for me. The game doesn’t shy away from the grim and eerie aspects of folklore, and it executes them in a way that’s both chilling and fascinating. You’re not just fighting monsters—you’re uncovering the tragic, often horrifying stories behind them.

Overall, this is one of the most immersive and unforgettable games I’ve ever played. Incredible storytelling, rich lore, and a hauntingly beautiful world—The Witcher 3 sets a standard that few games even come close to.

Once you get into the world of witcher 3 and its story, there's no turning back.

For me, this game is without a doubt number one. And I’m certain it will remain so.
A Polish studio of incredibly talented developers created a masterpiece that is forever written into the history of gaming.

Playing this game was part of a larger journey for me - I read all 9 of Andrzej Sapkowski’s books in the original language, and played through the entire trilogy in Polish as well. The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt became the perfect closing chapter of this unforgettable experience, ending it on an emotional high.

Everything about this game feels exceptional to me - the unmatched atmosphere and music, the characters and their personal stories. You can truly feel how much passion and love went into creating it.

I’m not someone who tends to analyze games from a purely technical standpoint - mechanics, systems, and so on - although in my opinion, those aspects are also well done here. For me, games are more about the experience you live through, something that stays with you long after you finish it.

And in that sense, The Witcher 3 reached deeper into my soul than any other game I’ve ever played.

I’m really looking forward to The Witcher 4 and new adventures - this time with Ciri. But I truly hope we’ll also get to see the beloved Geralt of Rivia again.

Review of base game only.

	It’s a pretty good game. Specifically it’s an 8/10 HBO miniseries with excellent acting and writing, that’s been somewhat clumsily grafted onto a mediocre action RPG. Combat is limp and poorly balanced, traversal is buggy and confusing (you’ll be constantly backtracking because environments are samey and the mini-map is useless), the crafting in this game is ABYSMALLY tedious, and loot is boring too. But this story, these characters, this world, is amazing.

	The lands of Novigrad, Velen, Skellige, and more, are rendered with an outstanding level of immersive believably. Geralt is such a strong character; he’s not super original but his writing meets the highest standard for an action protagonist. All the major side characters are complex and feel like they have so much more to them than what we get to see in game. The whole game, regardless of it’s massive size, feel like such small piece in it’s larger world. CDPR obviously had the luxury of having all of Andrzej Sapkowski’s books to work off of, but what they built on top of his creativity is strong too, and it really blows my mind more game studios haven’t tried adapting books to games like this. Writing is such a common low point in Gaming; what I wouldn’t give for The Witcher 3, the Wild Hunt, to be the baseline for video game storytelling.

	Gwent’s pretty cool too.

I can't really believe that this game was treated as an equally good game to Skyrim back in the day. In my opinion, The Witcher 3 is vastly superior in almost every aspect. The writing ranges from okay to amazing and majority of the time it sits comfortably in the upper end of that spectrum. The music is fantastic and the visuals are even more impressive considering the game is over a decade old by this point. The combat and gameplay never gets old, since the systems in place actually has some depth to them, but not too much as to overwhelm the player. Gwent, the in-game card game, is also super fun once you get the hang of it. I've never before seen a game introduce a completely optional mini-game that is better than some standalone games. 

It's not the deepest RPG out there in terms of choice and consequence, but there are often at least two outcomes to even simple sidequests. Some choices have unforeseen consequences and the game really does a good job of introducing this early on. 

The Witcher 3 is not a 'happily ever after' kind of game. It's gritty, brutal, and bittersweet. Many choices that seem to be the obvious pick often comes with a twist that makes them not so obvious in the end. So many games miss this level of nuance, that The Witcher 3 so often displays in its quest design. It makes you actually think about your choices and live with the consequences, instead of just making a clear cut choice that is the "good one" and then go on about your day. 

There is not a Baldur's Gate 3 level of interactivity here, but it doesn't set out to be that either. You are Geralt, a witcher, and the game really does immerse you in that role. It's a different kind of roleplaying experience but it allows for a story that hits hard and feels personal. Especially when you're playing on death march, where you'll be forced to use every tool at your disposal to be effective. 

Overall, The Witcher 3 is an amazing game and I enjoyed every minute of it. It's absolutely worth full price but the fact that it's dirt cheap every so often should definitely be enough to convince anyone to give it a try, if nothing else has by this point.

Meine Reise mit The Witcher 3 hat bereits 2015 auf der Xbox begonnen, und seitdem hat mich dieses Spiel nie wieder losgelassen. Ich habe es unzählige Male durchgespielt und trotzdem wurde es nie langweilig. Jedes Mal entdeckt man neue Details, neue Wege und neue Entscheidungen, die das Erlebnis verändern.

Später habe ich mir das Spiel auf GOG geholt und vor kurzem auch auf Steam. Besonders das Modding hat dem Spiel für mich nochmal eine ganz neue Dimension gegeben. Dinge, die auf der Konsole nicht möglich waren, machen das Erlebnis heute noch intensiver und abwechslungsreicher.

Was mich bis heute am meisten fasziniert, ist die unglaubliche Entscheidungsfreiheit. Es gibt selten nur „richtig“ oder „falsch“. Oft bewegt man sich irgendwo zwischen gut, besser oder moralisch fragwürdig. Und genau das macht die Welt so lebendig. Entscheidungen haben Konsequenzen, manchmal sofort, manchmal erst viele Stunden später. NPCs reagieren darauf, Gespräche verändern sich, und teilweise beeinflusst man sogar den gesamten Verlauf der Geschichte.

Die Spielwelt fühlt sich dadurch echt an. Man hört Gespräche, erlebt die Auswirkungen seines Handelns und merkt, dass die eigenen Entscheidungen Gewicht haben. Das ist etwas, das nur sehr wenige Spiele in dieser Tiefe schaffen.

Für mich ist The Witcher 3 nicht einfach nur ein Spiel, sondern eines der besten Rollenspiele aller Zeiten. Selbst nach all den Jahren macht es mir immer noch genauso viel Spaß wie am ersten Tag.

The Witcher 3 is an excellent RPG and easily sits at a 9/10, even just 12 hours in. It quickly shows why it’s so highly regarded, with strong storytelling and a rich world.

One of the biggest highlights is the writing. Quests feel meaningful, and even side missions have depth and interesting choices. The world feels alive, and characters are well-developed, making it easy to get invested.

Exploration is also very rewarding. There’s a lot to discover, from towns and wilderness to hidden quests and monsters. The atmosphere and music really help bring everything together.

Combat is decent, though not perfect. It works well enough, but it’s not the main reason to play the game. Some players might find it a bit repetitive over time.

Even early on, it’s clear the game offers a huge amount of content, and it only gets better as you progress.

Overall, The Witcher 3 is a fantastic experience with great storytelling and world-building. Even at 12 hours in, it’s easy to see why it’s considered one of the best RPGs.

The jack of all trades in the series. It doesn’t have the best main story (it’s dlcs are much better), nor the sublime atmosphere of the first game; but what it does have is a whole lot of meat to chew on. Side quests are well designed and usually lead to interesting choice consequences and hunting monsters is always a fun buildup to see what manner of beast (or not so beastly) you’ll be confronting. The combat is the best in the series but that’s still like saying the slow kid in class won the local special triathlon. Hit detection is wonky, and you’ll be fighting Geralt’s tank tread inertia more often than not when facing several foes. Inventory management is also clunky and not as fully thought out as I would’ve liked. Potion and oil management seems to have gotten a face lift since I last played back in 2015 on console, so there's that. 
Speaking of what else got a facelift, the next gen update added DX12 support which is great!…if it also didn’t come at the expense of halving your performance for ray tracing features that are better left turned off (unless you’re a lawyer rocking a high end rig in this day and age). I would advise anyone on lower or mid range hardware to launch the game in DX11, as you get the benefit of the visual overhaul without the middling performance from poor baked in GI implementation. 

Overall, it’s a good Witcher experience but far from it being a perfect one. Time has been kind on the major aspects of choice and story presentation but less so on the finer details of gameplay. The series still has a well of untapped potential to reach and hopefully the future will bring the level of…polish the series needs to improve.

My series ranking for anyone that cares: W1> HoS/B&W > W3 > W2

One of those very rare games where all of it's aspects are near perfect.
Story is fantastic, better than the books with side quests and DLCs that are as equally engulfing and compelling as the main story
Soundtrack is great, graphics are awesome (even 10+ years after the fact)
Gameplay is intuitive and easy to experiment different playstyles with, from the combat to the dialogue decisions, every iteration of your Geralt is well detailed and implemented.
And even outside of the actual game, it set the benchmark for positive AAA gaming standards (free content after release, continued consistent patching/modding support, substantial DLC that is reasonably priced, etc).
Imho, this is still CDPR's high point as a company, this game defined a generation and set numerous industry benchmarks. Highly recommend for anyone who hasn't tried it yet, it is one of my favorites.
--Testimony from someone who has a combined 400+ hours across multiple platforms over a decade

The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt is a game-changer for open-world RPGs. You play as Geralt of Rivia, a monster hunter on a quest to find his adopted daughter, Ciri. The story is engaging, with impactful choices, and the characters are well-written, making Geralt relatable and memorable.

The combat is smooth, combining swordplay with cool magic signs, plus there's a lot of customization for your playstyle. Exploring the stunning world is a joy, with each area packed with quests and secrets.

Visually, the game is gorgeous, with impressive detail and an awesome soundtrack that sets the mood. The side quests are some of the best around, offering rich stories that could easily stand as main quests in other games.

Overall, The Witcher 3 is an incredible experience that raises the bar for RPGs. It’s a must-play if you enjoy immersive stories and captivating worlds!

I bought the Witcher 3 10 years ago on PS4, played it for 30 minutes and decided it wasn't for me because the performance gave me headaches and I was too overwhelmed by the RPG mechanics and the dialogue heavy interactions. Fast forward to 2026 and I decided to give it another shot on PC for less than a fiver and I am I am so GLAD I played it again. To me, this isn't a video game but a really in depth and expansive choose your own adventure style book. Each quest is like a mini TV episode that really makes you think about morality, why people make certain choices and a lot of other complex philosophical dilemma's that left an impact on me and really made me think (which is rare for many video games nowadays as they are mostly dopamine button mashers). I think that this is easily the strongest immersion within a world that I have felt in a long time. However, the gameplay is pretty mid all things considered and quite easy. I played on death march and needed to reload a couple of times here and there but it was nothing that wasn't manageable. The combat is quite janky and animations aren't consistent which made it feel like I was fighting against the game at times. However, I did find it fun occasionally and especially fighting new and unique monsters with interesting backstories. Lastly the main quest story itself is alright, but the DLC is significantly better in my opinion. Hearts of stone has the perfect pacing and is easily my favourite DLC of all time. Overall, I highly recommend you play this if you prefer story and immersion over gameplay as the game wildly exceeds expectations in those areas. However, if you care about gameplay more then this may not be for you.

Playing this game as a grown man, vs. when I played the first time as a teenager, I still think this is beautiful game, but now understand [i] why [/i] it is so. There is no other game that is so drenched in dark fantasy, and dark fantasy done right. This is a master class in gritty & realistic environmental story-telling. You get to witness and influence a brutal war, glimpse the personalities and cultures of the inhabitants of the lands, fight a whole host of crazy monsters, and there is so much more in between it all.

I personally enjoy Gwent much more than I ever thought I would on paper. It's so well made for a minigame.

The DLC for this game are epic in every sense of the word, and I find them better than the base game in terms of story and the amount of fun I had, which is huge, because the base game is great. 

Overall, this game can be funny, it can be disturbing, it can be very intriguing.. 
It's like a novel you can play. Sometimes it comes across YA, sometimes romance novel, sometimes murder mystery. I've never played another story game where I've been so entrenched in the story like I'm watching a movie on the edge of my seat. Geraldo Rivera is a well written protagonist, and I like that we're given lots of choices, but no matter what you do, it seems also like what he would do. I think that's funny. Emotionless and yet somehow also a moral authority. The game really makes you feel like Batman. 

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Oh, and it seems they've added some free graphical and QOL updates since I last played, and they are all fantastic additions. Super appreciate not having to open the inventory of a plant to take ingredients out of it like a container. 

anyway thanks for reading bye

(p.s. I played 100 hours on Ps4)

The Witcher 3 – 200+ Hours Later and I Finally Found Ciri (But I Found Myself Too)

I booted up The Witcher 3 thinking, “I’ll just do a couple side quests.”
Cut to 100+ hours later, Geralt has a better skincare routine than I do, my moral compass is permanently bent, and I’m emotionally attached to at least three virtual people and one horse with questionable pathfinding.

This game doesn’t have side quests. It has main quests pretending to be side quests. You show up to kill a monster, and suddenly you’re mediating a divorce, uncovering a generational curse, and questioning the ethics of free will—for 12 XP and a pair of boots worse than the ones you’re wearing. And yet? Worth it. Every time.

Combat starts off as “button mashing with optimism” and slowly evolves into “pre-buffing like I’m about to file my taxes.” Oils, potions, decoctions—Geralt is basically a medieval chemist who drinks mystery liquids and calls it strategy. Sometimes I win fights through skill. Sometimes I win because Quen exists and I refuse to fight fairly.

The world is absurdly alive. Villages feel miserable in a charmingly authentic way. Everyone is dirty, sad, and deeply suspicious of you, which honestly feels right for a man who shows up armed to the teeth asking about drowners. Monsters aren’t just monsters—they’re metaphors, trauma responses, or the result of someone making a very bad life choice 30 years ago.

And then there’s Gwent. A side activity so powerful it briefly hijacks the entire genre. I did not save the world because I was noble. I saved it because that guy had a card I didn’t own.

After 100+ hours, the map is still full of question marks, my quest log is a philosophical essay, and I still occasionally fall to my death from a three-foot ledge. But that’s the magic of it. The Witcher 3 isn’t about completion—it’s about wandering, choosing badly, choosing better later, and occasionally punching a god because a contract said so.

Final verdict:
Come for the monster slaying.
Stay for the existential dread, elite card game addiction, and the realization that you would absolutely survive in a fantasy world by aggressively avoiding the main quest.

10/10. Would ignore Ciri again.

At first, combat feels fun. you swing, you dodge, you throw a sign, you’re like “yeah I got this”

then a griffin shows up. and you’re like, “ok, this is fine”

three potions later, you’re on the ground thinking about every life choice that led you here. fun, fair, but humbling. the game teaches patience, timing, and that your silver sword is not actually magic against every problem

side quests are insane. one minute you’re helping a farmer, next minute you’re solving a murder mystery with a ghost that roasts your detective skills. also, every NPC seems to have a story, and you end up caring way more than you expected. the game emotionally manipulates you, 10/10 would get attached to random villagers again

exploration is gorgeous. literally every corner has loot, monsters, or a story. you think you’re just walking to a signpost, suddenly you’re in a haunted tower, fighting for your life, and questioning why you left the inn

the leveling and gear systems are solid. skill points matter, potions matter, oils matter… basically, everything matters. ignore it and the game will politely murder you while you think you’re overpowered

and yes, I got salty. multiple times. multiple monsters, multiple choices, multiple cutscenes where Geralt has to explain why life is terrible. but it’s all fair. the game rewards learning and punishes mistakes in a way that makes sense, not cheap

end of the day:

combat is challenging but fair
world is enormous and beautiful
story is emotional and messy
side quests will steal your soul
Geralt is both your hero and your therapist

10/10
would get emotionally destroyed, fight a werewolf, lose 50 crowns, and still come back for more

This game is an absolute masterpiece.

1) Gameplay - The gameplay of this game is like a typical RPG where you get to make choices. But the consequences of your choices are very different which really makes it a very good RPG and makes the game re-playable to see the alternate consequences. The combat seems to be very clunky at first but once you play for some time you will definitely grasp it. There is a very good and proper armor and weapon system and upgrading them really gives you quite an advantage. Apart from that Geralt can cast some spells and use certain bombs which helps defeating certain formidable foes. Also he can brew potions and decoctions and using them gives him some time-limited status effects. The enemies are also very diverse and require different strategies. Although the fighting is old style but it's but very fun and fulfills its objective in the game. And roach, your riding horse makes traversal across huge map quite easy. Also you can use fast travel by discovering signposts or from boats to harbors. And the most important is gwent, a standalone card-game inside the game which has a potential to be a standalone game in itself. Every witcher player mustn't miss out on gwent. Also there are some mini activities available where you can pass your time. This game is quite slow-paced because it expects you to explore the world and do side quests which give you amazing rewards. I definitely recommend the players not to rush the game as it destroy the experience and enjoyment.

2) World - The world of witcher 3 is very vibrant and alive and open world which gives freedom to explore. Each region has its own vibe, atmosphere, people, creatures and culture. It really feels as if you're living in that world. There are quite good landmarks as well which are memorable.

3) Story and characters - This game has a very good story in the base game as well as in the expansions( one of the greatest). I definitely recommend you play all. Even the side quests are so well written that they have the potential to challenge many modern games main story. This has inspired many later games. The game has lot of different memorable characters with unique traits. Talking with them feels like you're talking with real people not some robots. Even Geralt is a well written and potrayed character which feels human even witchers seem to lack emotions. A lot of the charcters have their side quests which gives more insights about them. Also you'll find a lot of books which provide significant lore about the witcher universe the game is based on. Since it's an RPG, your decisions have consequences in the story and will give you different endings.

4) Art-style and Graphics - The visuals of this game is stunning and jaw-dropping. The color palettes of different regions are different. The next-gen update even improved the visual experience further. The art style reflects the vibes of a dark fantasy world.

5) Music - One of the best part of the game. The music of this game really steals your heart. I would say they are one of the best music in the gaming industry. One can spend hours and hours being idle and listening to the music.

I recommend this game highly to RPG lovers, fantasy world lovers, open world explorers and story game enjoyers.

Story & Writing: It offers an emotional, high-stakes narrative with morally complex, "grey" choices where no option is strictly right or wrong. Even minor side quests feature deep storytelling, often exceeding the quality of other games' main plots.
World Building: The world is living and breathing, filled with incredible detail, rich lore, and atmospheric environments that reward exploration.
Characters: Geralt is an established, compelling protagonist, surrounded by a memorable supporting cast, including strong female characters like Yennefer and Ciri.
Content & Value: The game is massive, offering over 100 hours of content, complemented by two exceptional expansions (Hearts of Stone and Blood and Wine).

I usually don’t review games but this one… man this game is something else.

Everything about it is just insane — the music is god tier, the story pulls you in like crazy, and even the side quests feel better than full games out there. Side quests are so good they rival many games’ main stories. I legit didn’t expect to get this invested.

The world feels alive, like actually alive. Characters feel real, voice acting is perfect, and you actually care about what’s happening. It’s not just playing a game, you actually feel like you’re in it.

I don’t even have the right words to explain how good this experience was. No game has made me feel like this in a long time.

Having played The Witcher 2 before Witcher 3, I can confidently say that Witcher 3 is like Witcher 2 but on steroids. Almost the same mechanics but a bit less clunky, amazing storyline, and most importantly the graphics. Walking (or riding) around the world and seeing the breathtaking environment of this game is such a please on my eye. I also played this game throughout summer last year and it was a blast. Excited to play this game again soon!

The Witcher 3 is one of those rare games that completely pulls you into its world. The story is emotional and mature, the side quests are better written than most main stories in other games, and every choice feels like it actually matters. Combat blends swordplay, magic, and alchemy, and while it can feel clunky at first, it becomes rewarding once you learn the systems. The soundtrack and atmosphere elevate the experience even more.

Comes with 2 DLCs, which to call them that would be an insult, since I'd call them almost stand-alone games, capable of captivating you on their own.

It delivers one of the most engaging and emotional RPG experiences available, making it a must-play for fantasy fans.

Witcher 3 and its dlc are the best RPG experience you can have on a handheld. I was able to play through this game entirely on my Legion GO s Z1E on Medium - High settings at 40-50 fps without issue. It was such a great experience that I would recommend this game to anyone just for the story alone. Blood and wine is by far the best dlc in any video game that I have ever played. The only issue Witcher 3 has that its combat system is very dated. I would highly recommend anyone who plays this game to start on either Broken Bones or Death March to get the actual Witcher experience and even these aren't that difficult.
